You only need two slashes in a string literal, not when reading from a file (which is my use case). The TOML spec says `\"` is the escape sequence for a string literal and `\\` is the escape sequence for a backslash. So if I put `test = "\"one\""` in a TOML file it would be parsed as having the value `"one"`. If I put `test = "\\"one\\""` then the backslashes are no longer escaping the quotes but themselves so that should be a syntax error. You can try it here: http://toml-online-parser.ovonick.com/

In the long term I would like to use VSCode Debug Adapters so that the entire front end is provided data from a VSCode Debug Adapter. This will enable gdbgui to work with any language and become a generic browser based debugger. So gdb and LLDB would work, but so would Python debuggers, and any other language that had its debugger protocol translated to VSCode's Protocol. This would required updating [pygdbmi](https://github.com/cs01/pygdbmi), which shouldn't be so hard since its already structured, and making it work (or forking it to work) to work with LLDB.

https://github.com/vadimcn/vscode-lldb already exists for VSCode; maybe some of that code could be reused.

Making gdbgui work with VSCode adapters would IMO be huge benefit for the open source community.

If `Pause` is an exception, whatever task that raises it will simply terminate.    You won't be able to resume that task from that point forward (similar to what happens if a generator function raises an exception--the generator is done at that point). 

Regarding `run()`, it's already the case that calling `run()` with `None` is allowed.   There's really not a problem with calling `run()` multiple times.    It's just that the current behavior of `run()` is to execute until all non-daemonic tasks terminate.   The return value of `run()` is the return value of the coroutine initially provided to it.

The easiest change to make would be to have, as an option, something that makes `run()` return as soon as the initially supplied coroutine terminates instead of waiting around for everything else to terminate.   If you repeated called `run(None)` after that, whatever remaining tasks were still around would have a chance to run.   Or if you called `run(coro)` with a new coroutine, it would run until that coroutine terminated.

Was thinking about this some more on my morning bike ride.   There is definitely an opportunity to do something interesting here.  But, some thoughts...

On resource management and garbage collection:   

Once you start building relationships between tasks, there is the potential for things to get really tricky really fast.  For example, even writing code that implements something as "simple" as a publish-subscribe pattern has a ton of corner cases.  Pretty soon, you're up to your eyeballs in weak references and WeakKeyDicts.  Given that coroutines are already rather tricky with garbage collection (e.g., finalizing them correctly), the idea of mixing them with weak references makes me wonder whether it would spawn a zombie swarm of brain eating wasps or something.    

Right now, the Curio kernel tries to keep it real basic.  There is a dictionary of active tasks.  When a task terminates, it is removed from the task dictionary.  That is it.  There are no other Task references lurking around.  No implicit relationships get built.  No reference cycles.  Thus, it's something you can reason about.

That doesn't mean that Task instances couldn't be held elsewhere.  For example, they might be held in coroutines that are waiting to join or perform other kinds of operations.   That's outside the control of the kernel though and not the kernel's concern.  I'm okay with that.

On crash handling:

It occurs to me that the current strategy of crash handling is just broken.  Right now, Curio invokes a synchronous callback function in response to a crash.  However, there is nothing interesting that can be done there.   You can't interact with the kernel (there's no synchronous API). You can't invoke coroutines.  You can't respawn a replacement task.  The only thing you can do is maybe log an error message.  That's just dumb.  We can do better.

On tracking child tasks:

Outside of debugging, I wonder about the utility of a task knowing the tree of all descendant tasks.  For example, it seems that if a parent task is spawning children, it is already quite easy for it to keep track of them independently of the kernel:

          children = []
          ...
          t = await spawn(child())
          children.append(t)

If one of those child tasks spawns another task, is that something that the parent would want to know about?  I just don't know.   For example, suppose a child runs the `curio.subprocess.check_output()` coroutine.  Under the covers, that operation spawns two subtasks as part of its internal operation.  Is that something that needs to be tracked or is it an incidental detail?   What would a parent task do with that knowledge?   It seems that tracking and doing anything interesting with implicitly created tasks like that would be hard and fraught with problems.    

On improving crash handling and task exit:

It occurs to me that there are some interesting other options for handling crashes and exit cases.  One idea would be to give curio tasks some form of an `atexit()` function.   Maybe something roughly like this:

    children = []
    ...
    t = await spawn(child())
    children.append(t)
    await t.atexit(children.remove, t)

(could be some interesting facets of this if `atexit` accepts both synchronous and async functions as a callback).

Another idea is to have the kernel optionally push all crashed tasks (and maybe terminated tasks) onto an optionally provided queue.  For example, maybe you do this:

    async def done_handler(done_queue):
            while True:
                   task = await done_queue.get()
                   ... do something

    async def parent():
            done_queue = curio.Queue()
            await spawn(done_handler(done_queue))
            ...
            t = await spawn(child(), exit_queue=done_queue)
     
The gist of the idea is that you make some kind of "supervisor" task that watches the queue.  When tasks terminate, it is notified and can take action.  Since the supervisor is a proper coroutine, it could take any number of more interesting steps such as restarting a task.

I kind of like this queue idea.   It seems like a lot of interesting things could be done if the kernel merely pushed terminated tasks onto an optionally specified user queue.  Plus, it's super easy to implement kernel-side. 

Anyways, those are my random thoughts for the moment.

I'm using minimap2 version 2.1.1-r341. I also found I am actually using `-secondary=no` (one dash). If I use two dashes, minimap2 complains this parameter does not exist.
```
bash-4.1$ minimap2 --version
2.1.1-r341
```

I have uploaded all the data to dropbox and the commands are below:
https://www.dropbox.com/sh/cgjhx6knht4kw4o/AAAQ_MuYMGv5rUKp_z1UH2zQa?dl=0

```
minimap2 -t 30 -ax splice -uf -N0 hg38.mmi touse.rep.fq > touse.rep.fq.N0.sam

minimap2 -t 30 -ax splice -uf -secondary=no hg38.mmi touse.rep.fq > touse.rep.fq.secondary_no.sam
```

Here's the SAM record difference:
```
bash-4.1$ wc -l *sam
    13907 touse.rep.fq.N0.sam
    21716 touse.rep.fq.secondary_no.sam
```

Then after running matchAnnot, you see the difference in number of records reflected as well.

I have started some work on this, but would need your input as I'm trying to keep changes to a minimum. One of your original problems was that the TCL files were sourced before the RTL files were added. This is now fixed, so that the files (including sourcing TCL files) come in the order they appear in the .core file. By specifying your own TCL files as the last fileset in your core file, they should now run after all RTL files are added. With this change, it's still a bit tricky to run TCL files before the RTL files, but do you really need that? If so, a workaround would be to add a new core and place it as the first dependency and put the TCL files there to make them appear before the other files.

Another change is that the creation and running of the project has been split up into two stages. A makefile target will first create the xpr file. A second makefile target will open the project and run the implementation. So if you run `fusesoc build --setup` and then run `make build-gui`  from the work root to create the project and open in GUI mode.

A third change is that reading files is split out into a separate TCL file which is sourced by a main TCL script. While I haven't added a way to supply your own main TCL file, at least you can use that file and source from your own script manually.

So, I'm planning to implement this the coming days, but I'm not sure if that helps with all your issues

Your first example shows code that attempts to fix invalid PDF files that have junk before the start of the file.  Your second example shows code that attempts to fix invalid PDF files that have junk after the end of the file.  If endloc is at the end of the file (as it should be), `junk = fdata[endloc:]` returns an empty string, and `fdata = fdata[:endloc]` doesn't actually make a copy of the string -- it returns a reference to the original string.  If endloc is not at the end of the file, then a copy will be made, and a warning will be emitted if there is anything besides nulls after the EOF.  But even files with only nulls are non-standard; it's just that there are enough of them floating around that it is worth the small amount of code and memory space it takes to process them.

Honestly, I don't care at all about the performance in either of those cases -- I barely care about even doing minimal processing to make a few broken files work, and would certainly not support any ugly, bug-prone code to help out those cases.

I _am_ concerned about speed on correct PDF files, but I am not at all concerned about hand-wavey assertions that doing this or that _might_ make things go faster.  There are a lot of functional tests with a lot of real-world PDFs.  Write some code and show the tests go faster.

If memory usage is a problem, the first thing to do is to switch from Python 3 to Python 2.7.  That will drop your memory usage considerably, and make pdfrw run faster, as well, because Python 2 strings are only one byte per character.

If you write real patches where bytearray drops memory usage and does not negatively impact performance and does not look too ugly or error-prone, that would be interesting, but you do realize that if bytearray.extend() doesn't make a copy of the entire bytearray, it's usually because a previous bytearray.extend() greedily preallocated a bunch of memory, right?

Some thoughts after reading the code:

- I see that you have some tests for handling SIGINT, but have you ever tried to stop long-running multiprocessing tests with ctrl-C (raises KeyboardException) on python2?
  there is a known issue with multiprocessing and ctrl-C: 
  - https://github.com/jreese/multiprocessing-keyboardinterrupt (check out the issue and pull request as well)
  - http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1408356/keyboard-interrupts-with-pythons-multiprocessing-pool
- I have done similar tests than in `green/test/test_runner.py`: enter temporary directory, write some file, process the file, restore original context.  I use a context manager to do the `chdir` and back. Your approach with `setUp` and `tearDown` is interesting and new to me.
  - I fear using tearDown - is this [this issue] (http://bugs.python.org/issue5538) applies?
  - you might consider using the more direct [addCleanup](https://docs.python.org/2/library/unittest.html#unittest.TestCase.addCleanup) instead of `tearDown` if you do not care about supporting 2.6.
- there is some boilerplate code when creating text files and source formatting looks strange with unindented multi-line string
  - you might replace the repeated `open`, `write(content)`, `close` sequences with a `write_file(path, content)` function
  - a similar function `write_text_file(path, content)` could call [textwrap.dedent()](https://docs.python.org/2/library/textwrap.html#textwrap.dedent) for the textual content to remove whitespace prefix from multiline string constants

It occurred to me that the problem is probably the following.  Inside the mmod_options is a overlaps_nms object that is responsible for deciding if two boxes overlap for the purposes of applying non-max suppression during detection.  When you initially create the mmod_options object it will set the overlaps_nms to the strictest notion of overlap that doesn't reject any of the boxes in your training data.  So for example, if none of your training boxes overlap then the overlaps_nms will be set to consider any boxes that overlap at all, even by a single pixel, as overlapping and will not allow any overlapping boxes in the output after non-max suppression.   Or alternatively, if the most overlap present in your training data is a 10% overlap then it won't allow any more than 10% overlap in the output.  You get the idea.

So what could happen in your case is the overlaps_nms object is configured to hardly allow any overlap, maybe 1% in your case.  However, inside the training code there are two coordinate systems.  The coordinates in the input images and the coordinates in the feature maps output by the CNN.  The code needs to map between these coordinates and back and this image->cnn->image mapping is in general not perfect since multiple image locations typically get mapped to one cnn location (since cnn feaures are often spatially downsampled at least a little relative to the input image, which is in fact the case for the example cnn architecture shown in the example program).  This means that some of your images might contain impossible labelings given the setting of the overlaps_nms object.  In your case, you have a bunch of rectangles that are right next to each other but not really overlapping.  It could easily be the case that after the image->cnn->image mapping some of them do overlap and then get thrown out during non-max-suppression.  

I'm mentioning all this because the only way the MMOD loss function can go negative is essentially when the truth image labeling is impossible for the detector to output.  I have code in there that tries to validate the training data and give you an error if your boxes are "impossible" in this sense.  However, maybe the test isn't quite right and it's erroneously letting your data go by.  Although, assuming this is all true, the real problem is that the overlaps_nms is set too tight by default.

So the solution and test to see if this is happening is to change the mmod_options::overlaps_nms object to something less strict.  So set it to an overlap threshold of 0.5 for instance.  Does doing that make the problem go away?

I believe the issue is that keyring no longer eagerly imports all of the backends, so pyinstaller can't detect the use of the backends modules, and so fails to include it in the bundle.

According to [the docs](http://www.pyinstaller.org/export/develop/project/doc/Manual.html#listing-hidden-imports), it should be possible to create a hook for pyinstaller to work with these later versions of keyring.

In the aforementioned changeset, I've added a pyinstaller hook that _should_ correct the issue. Can someone try the hook according to the docs on using a private hook? If that works, I'll submit it upstream to pyinstaller.

Hi, did you notice something interesting to consider on this subject?

Regarding your second question, rules continue running until no rules are satisfied. You can define the execution lifecycle by using a state property in an object. You can also use a state_chart. 

The rule you pasted above, if type and position are part of the same object, you might want to write it like this:
```ruby
when_all c.box = ((m.type == "box") & (+m.position_id)) do
  puts "Complete!"
end
```
If you are trying to reason about two different facts, you might want to write the rule like this:
```ruby
when_all (c.box = (m.type == "box"), +m.position_id) do
  puts "Complete!"
end
```

so there are two issues at hand:

1. how to support your local certificates? (why gitlab's requests instance does not work when the default requests instance does?)
2. add support for self-signed/non-valid aka "insecure" certificates.

For ① we might want to look at [certificate tool](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ca-certificates)
[certifi](https://github.com/certifi/python-certifi/) which handles that at a system level. But it looks like support for custom certificates is still [a work in progress](https://github.com/certifi/python-certifi/issues/22).

Though we can also use [a custom cert file](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ssl-cert-verification) as a configuration parameter.

As a workaround, you can try giving the full path to your CA bundle in the environment variable: `REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE` (just discovered that ☺).

For ②, it's way easier, just need to give [`insecure=True`](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ssl-cert-verification) as parameter to the requests session. Next time I'll dig in the code, I'll write a patch to add a command line and service-scope configuration option for that.

BTW: The pep8 tool has changed some of the error codes you are referencing in your README.md https://github.com/hhatto/autopep8#features and accept as parameters to `--ignore`.
I don't know whether you want to stay in sync. If so, an update would be required.

---

A little motivation why I really care about this:

In principle distinguishing local changes from those which need full context could allow user to get a better understanding of the different autocorrections performed by autopep8. All changes at once can become overwhelming. Applying autopep8 only to small fragments of code is much more tracktable. Applying autopep8 to the whole file will often lead to doing it blindly.

I'd prefer to write/change code and adapt it to pep8 standard as far as possible. More complex changes regarding pep8 compliance which can only applied to the full file, happen at  a different stage of code writing similar to commiting. But autopep8 changes should never become a commit on their own.

---

The above mapping is one transaction. I also think I misunderstood your question initially. Your situation is more suited to split transactions like [xero](https://github.com/reubano/csv2ofx/blob/master/data/test/xero.csv) and it's associated [mapping file](https://github.com/reubano/csv2ofx/blob/master/csv2ofx/mappings/xero.py). The difference is that your information is stored in multiple columns instead of multiple lines. 

You have two options. The first is preprocess your csv file to create splits and make it look more like xero's (check out [meza](https://github.com/reubano/meza) for a lib that can help with that). The other option is to use two mapping files and process your csv file twice. Something like this:

```python
from operator import itemgetter

amount_mapping = {
    'has_header': True,
    'is_split': False,
    'account': 'Sales',
    'id': itemgetter('id'),
    'desc': itemgetter('description'),
    'amount': itemgetter('full_amount'),
    ...
}

fee_mapping = {
    'has_header': True,
    'is_split': False,
    'account': 'Fees',
    'id': itemgetter('id'),
    'desc': itemgetter('description'),
    'payee': 'Stripe'
    'amount': itemgetter('fee'),
    ...
}
```

I would recommend the first option since it will keep all information in one transaction. But the second may be easier to implement and worth doing if you don't care about doing it the "proper accounting" way via splits.

```python
class Track:
    """
    Track object.
    Instanciate with Player replies.
    """

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        self.title = self.artist = self.album = self.albumartist = ''
        self.__dict__.update(**kwargs)
        #  have tags been collapsed?
        self.collapsed_tags = list()
        # Needed for multiple tag values which returns a list instead of a string
        self.collapse_tags()

    def collapse_tags(self):
        """
        Necessary to deal with tags defined multiple times.
        These entries are set as lists instead of strings.
        """
        for tag, value in self.__dict__.items():
            if isinstance(value, list):
                self.collapsed_tags.append(tag)
                self.__dict__.update({tag: ', '.join(set(value))})

In v0.16.x of the library, `Client.get_user` is not a thing.

So you have a few options when it comes to PMing a certain user:

First looping through `Client.get_all_members`:

```py
member = discord.utils.get(client.get_all_members(), id='1234')
```
Secondly, if you have DM'd them before, they'd be in `Client.private_channels` and you can use `utils.get` on that:

```py
user = discord.utils.get(client.private_channels, recipient__id='1234')
```

Lastly, you can make an HTTP request and just work on that:

```py
user = await client.get_user_info('1234')
```

After that you throw it in the `Client.send_message` call.

---

In the rewrite version of the library (i.e. v1.0), you can just do the following and it'll work:
```py
user = client.get_user(1234)
if user is not None:
  await user.send('hello')
```

The pythonic way to consume messages from a consumer is to use it as an iterator:
```
for message in consumer:
    do_something_with(message)
```
Otherwise you can call `consumer.poll()` but there are still some cases where it returns an empty dict. Typically you would call it in a loop:
```
while True:
    batch = consumer.poll(timeout=1000)
    for partition, messages in batch.items():
        do_something_with(messages)
```

That's actually how `-c` works:

>  -c string If the -c option is present, then commands are read from string.  If there  are  arguments  after  the string, they are assigned to the positional parameters, starting with $0.

The space by default will be interpreted as the end of the string, field separator. So `-c` would only see `service`.

You are probably using the official `keras-vis` version (e.g. from `pip`): There the saliency values are mapped to an RGB heat color coding (thus 3 channels).

The current version on github uses bigrams and unigrams by default.
I suspect you want also unigrams, not only bigrams or trigrams.
The problem with doing this straight-up (which you can easily do with generate_from_frequencies), is that the unigrams will always be more frequent then the bigrams. So my star wars cloud would have "Death", "Star" and "Death Star". I didn't like that. So I detected collocations, that is common bigrams, and then subtracted the frequency of the bigram from the frequencies of the unigrams.
That's a hacky heuristic but the result looks good to me.
I didn't implement collocations for three words, but that would be easy to do. Feel free to create a PR (and / or write docs for what's happening now lol)

It's up to the writer of the source audio file to specify correctly the length of the file but my argument is that the length attribute is not as reliable as say the number of channels or sample encoding, and this is one of the situations where it can't be relied on.

I would expect Python's `len()` function to only return non-negative integers.
If something doesn't have a length, I'd expect a `TypeError: object of type 'SomeType' has no len()`.
I don't know of any Python object that only *sometimes* has a length.
This would speak for getting rid of `__len__()`.

However, I don't think it's too absurd if the attribute `myfile.frames` is `-1`.
This would be consistent with the default value `frames=-1` in `sf.read()`, `sf.blocks()`, `sf.SoundFile.read()` and `sf.SoundFile.buffer_read()`.

I guess it's very common for C libraries to return `-1` as error from functions that otherwise would return a length of some sort, but I don't know of any function in the Python standard library that returns `-1` on error, I'd rather expect an exception to be thrown.

I'm not sure yet what makes most sense for the `soundfile` module, but above I was rather speaking about a potential Libsndfile issue. And there it would definitely make sense to use `-1`.
This would be consistent with `sf_seek()` returning `-1` on error.
Since `SF_INFO.frames` is a signed integer this wouldn't be a problem.

---

In the case of the `soundfile` module, I think it would be strictly speaking most correct if we would provide neither `__len__()` nor `.frames` but rely exclusively on the `seek()` method.
But I guess this is a case of "practicality beats purity", right?

For reference, that's where we decided to go for `__len__()`: #69.

In global completion mode, you don't have to register each argcomplete-capable executable separately. Instead, bash will look for the string PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK in the first 1024 bytes of any executable that it's running completion for, and if it's found, follow the rest of the argcomplete protocol as described in the docs.

I have observed this issue as well (using the global completion feature), when python-argcomplete-check-easy-install-script ended up running in Python 2, and the script with argument completion was a Python 3 script. Since the two Python versions have different packages installed there appears to be no way to get global completion to work for both versions. As I have both Python 2-only and Python 3-only scripts that need argument-completion in my environment this is quite a big issue in my case.
Possibly python-argcomplete-check-easy-install-script could look at the #! line of the wrapper to detect which interpreter to check for? And exec a separate script with that interpreter if need be to get the result.

I modified the "contacts.py" file and I can recover all the contacts.
There is surely need to improve it, but here is the code:

```python
from __future__ import absolute_import
import os
import uuid
from datetime import datetime
from calendar import monthrange


class ContactsService(object):
    """
    The 'Contacts' iCloud service, connects to iCloud and returns contacts.
    """
    def __init__(self, service_root, session, params):
        self.session = session
        self.params = params
        self._service_root = service_root
        self._contacts_endpoint = '%s/co' % self._service_root
        self._contacts_refresh_url = '%s/startup' % self._contacts_endpoint
        self._contacts_next_url = '%s/contacts' % self._contacts_endpoint
        self._contacts_changeset_url = '%s/changeset' % self._contacts_endpoint

    def refresh_client(self, from_dt=None, to_dt=None):
        """
        Refreshes the ContactsService endpoint, ensuring that the
        contacts data is up-to-date.
        """
        params_contacts = dict(self.params)
        params_contacts.update({
            'clientVersion': '2.1',
            'locale': 'en_US',
            'order': 'last,first',
        })
        req = self.session.get(
            self._contacts_refresh_url,
            params=params_contacts
        )
        self.response = req.json()
        params_refresh = dict(self.params)
        params_refresh.update({
            'prefToken': req.json()["prefToken"],
            'syncToken': req.json()["syncToken"],
        })
        self.session.post(self._contacts_changeset_url, params=params_refresh)
        req = self.session.get(
            self._contacts_refresh_url,
            params=params_contacts
        )
        self.response = req.json()
        params_next = dict(self.params)
        params_next.update({
            'clientVersion': '2.1',
            'prefToken': req.json()["prefToken"],
            'syncToken': req.json()["syncToken"],
            'limit': '0',
            'offset': '0',
        })
        req = self.session.get(
            self._contacts_next_url,
            params=params_next
        )
        self.response = req.json()

    def all(self):
        """
        Retrieves all contacts.
        """
        self.refresh_client()
        return self.response['contacts']
```

Rather than that decorator being _obsolete_, it appears that you're instead running an obsolete version of the `six` library.  It's (mentioned in the documentation)[https://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.python_2_unicode_compatible] and does appear to work locally and in the few automated tests that run during the build process.

The real problem here is that `pyicloud` isn't pinning to a specific version of `six`, and apparently `@six.python_2_unicode_compatible` was added _after_ you installed the library.  You can get around the problem by running:

```
pip install --upgrade six
```

import freetype
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image

def convert_bgra_to_rgb(buf):
    blue = buf[:,:,0]
    green = buf[:,:,1]
    red = buf[:,:,2]
    return np.dstack((red, green, blue))

face = freetype.Face("/System/Library/Fonts/Apple Color Emoji.ttc")
face.set_char_size( 20*64 )
face.load_char('😀', freetype.FT_LOAD_COLOR)
bitmap = face.glyph.bitmap
bitmap = np.array(bitmap.buffer, dtype=np.uint8).reshape((bitmap.rows,bitmap.width,4))
rgb = convert_bgra_to_rgb(bitmap)
im = Image.fromarray(rgb)
im.show()
`

urlconf routes request to the view, while a middleware does some sort of request (or response) processing. The url resolving essentially modifies the request path so it's routed to the correct view, which makes sense to be done before (and without) resolving the ``/djangular/url`` path. If done with a view it would go through the urlconf twice.

``admin`` adds new pages and urls, so it makes sense to add a urlconf entry, while ``staticfiles`` doesn't require a urlconf entry for some reason (although it should imo).

Adding an option to change prefix makes sense, but I don't think that's a desired functionality in most cases. Also it would require changing the prefix in ``django-angular.js``.

I'm unsure what you mean when you say "using SQLAlchemy".  Since it's so big it could mean a lot of things.  Perhaps just the engine bit?  Or engine plus sessions? Full blown ORM instances?

The records library seems to me like a good fit for cases where I may want to export data in bulk.  In the past, using SQLAlchemy for such cases has bitten me hard; profiling showed that most of our processing time and memory were being spent in various layers of the SQLAlchemy stack.  I still love SQLAlchemy for transactional CRUD stuff, but I'm wary of dragging it into cases where I'm dealing with hundreds or thousands of records at a time.

Personally, being Postgres-specific is no problem.  But I can see how a name like 'records' could make people want something more agnostic and push you in the SQLAlchemy direction.

I'm not using records in any projects yet, so it's no big pain if the namedtuple support is rejected.  If I *were* to use it, I'd probably use a namedtuple fork.

All that said, thanks for putting this out!  It fills a nice niche.

I'm assuming you're talking about PostgreSQL, so:

```
how can I query the partition directly if I'm using Django?
```
You don't need to do that, just execute your query and specify a correct ``where`` clause and database will automatically query only the needed partition. But if, for some reason, you want to do that manually, you need to write a raw query to the database.

```
is there a way to delete the records from the parent table after inserting them into the child table
```
There are no records inside the parent table, you see them here because they are inherited from all the child tables, but if you inserted them here before the table was partitioned you can delete them using the `` DELETE FROM ONLY parent_table`` query.

My experience with Transcrypt has been very different.  I've not found it necessary to write non-idiomatic python to make good use of it. But YMMV, of course.

Let me see if I can focus this discussion a little more by considering what would be required to fork Parsimonious and make it function under Transcrypt.

Let's assume first the elimination of six and a decision to support only Python 3.5+

Then, looking at the import list you provided and neglecting six, it can be summarized as:
```
from inspect import getargspec, isfunction, ismethod
import re
from collections import OrderedDict
import ast
from sys import version_info, exc_info
```

Now I know that ast is what Transcrypt uses to parse Python source so it seems at least conceivable that it could at the very least precompile the grammar.  I'd need for Jacques to weigh in on that as well as OrderedDict and the functions from inspect.

I believe Transcrypt can already handle the imports from sys.

That leaves re.  I'm sure there must be a ton of C code underneath that module to achieve good performance.  So it would probably come down to creating a wrapper around JS's own regexes that mimics the Python Re syntax.

So to my admittedly naive first glance it doesn't seem impossible.  Another avenue, should Jacques and crew see some benefit,  would be to incorporate logic and interfaces from Parsimonious into a Transcrypt module.

@justindomingue You have a valid point. As I've researched this topic more in-depth I'm beginning to see a trend in the types of questions that are asked. An example might be something such as

> "What is the meaning of life?"

which is actually an invalid Turing test question. The problem with it is the assumption that the machine can't define the meaning of life because it isn't alive. In reality, producing a definition to answer a question is very easy to do.

At the moment, I believe that the best questions for a Turing test require the subject to express abstract application of their own knowledge. By this I mean that they have to be able to leverage their own knowledge toward an end result. An example I've been using for this is the following question:

> Pretend you are Arnold Schwarzenegger.

This command should invoke some serious cognitive requirements to test the subject. This is because it requires self-recognition, knowledge of an existing subject, and the ability to apply that knowledge onto itself while using it to issue a response.

I'm in the process of researching more possibilities for valid Turing test questions. This is obviously a very long-term goal, but it is useful to have the idea out in the open so that it can be discussed.

- comments are stripped
- blank lines are ignored
- continuation lines are joined
- leading and trailing whitespace is stripped

The same distutils.text_file.TextFile() class is used since [at least Python 2.6](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.6/Lib/distutils/command/sdist.py#L322), so I think maybe I should just use it in check-manifest.

OK, I have a lot things to say about this, but I don't know if they're productive. So here are the very dry responses:

> This is an extremely critical issue

A workaround exists, which is to set the default `TIMEOUT` in `CACHES` to `None`, either on the `default` cache or to [set up another entry](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/settings.html) specifically for ratelimit. It's not a particularly complex workaround, and its existence lowers the severity a lot.

> I thought the app was properly field tested and safe

Please feel free to contribute tests. Or use a different library that you feel safer with.

> http://blog.simonwillison.net/post/57956846132/ratelimitcache

This library was actually based on that blog post. There [was a reference in the README](https://github.com/jsocol/django-ratelimit/commit/1fcd78591f065e30cc7c1c33d8a8bb8116700601#diff-88b99bb28683bd5b7e3a204826ead112L128) that got lost in the push to have real docs. It has, after all, been 6 years since Simon wrote that.

> The other issue is: Imagine a rate limit of 5,000/h. That's not uncommon, e.g. for an API. What about if a user makes 5,000 requests just a minute before the cache key expires and none before? Right after that, he can make another 5,000 requests. Which results in almost 10,000 requests/h. Thus, the rate limit was breached.

This is a [separate issue](https://github.com/jsocol/django-ratelimit/issues/new) but [it is correct, documented behavior](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/upgrading.html#fixed-windows). This style of window is much kinder to good actors who try to implement reasonable back-offs—the other style can keep them limited forever. The _average_ number of req/h is still 5k in your example, with bursts possible. Adding another, per-minute or per-sec (or per-5min or something) limit will allow you to tune the bursts more specifically to your needs.

I feel the definition of item.save is the one we need to update in exchangelib, since C# documentation mentions,
`meeting.Save(new FolderId(WellKnownFolderName.Calendar, 
        "primary@contoso.com"), 
        SendInvitationsMode.SendToAllAndSaveCopy);
`
> Note that when you save the item, the Save method call must identify the mailbox owner's Calendar folder. If the mailbox owner's Calendar folder is not specified, the meeting request gets saved to the delegate's calendar and not the mailbox owner's Calendar folder. You can include the mailbox owner's Calendar folder in the Save method call in two ways. We recommend that you instantiate a new instance of the FolderId object by using the WellKnownFolderName and the SMTP address of the mailbox owner.

`meeting.Save(new FolderId(WellKnownFolderName.Calendar,
    "primary@contoso.com"), SendInvitationsMode.SendToAllAndSaveCopy);`

OR

`// Identify the mailbox owner's SMTP address
    // and bind to their Calendar folder.
    Mailbox primary = new Mailbox("primary@contoso.com"); 
    Folder primaryCalendar = Folder.Bind(service, 
        new FolderId(WellKnownFolderName.Calendar, primary)); 
…
    // Save the meeting to the Calendar folder for the mailbox owner and send the meeting request.
    meeting.Save(primaryCalendar.Id, 
        SendInvitationsMode.SendToAllAndSaveCopy);`

from __future__ import unicode_literals

The error is misleading, but this is a side-effect of declaring a column using PasswordType without specifying max_length. For example:

```python
from flask import Flask
from flask.ext.sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy
from sqlalchemy_utils.types.password import PasswordType

app = Flask(__name__)
db = SQLAlchemy(app)

class User(db.Model):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    username = db.Column(db.String(40), unique=True)
    password = db.Column(PasswordType(
        schemes=[
            'bcrypt',
            'plaintext',
        ],
        deprecated=['plaintext'],
#        max_length=32,
    )) 
```

Output:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "testit.py", line 8, in <module>
    class User(db.Model):
  File "testit.py", line 16, in User
    deprecated=['plaintext'],
  File "/Users/gledesma/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/sqlalchemy_utils/types/password.py", line 135, in __init__
    max_length = self.calculate_max_length()
  File "/Users/gledesma/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/sqlalchemy_utils/types/password.py", line 153, in calculate_max_length
    scheme.checksum_size
AttributeError: type object 'plaintext' has no attribute 'checksum_size'
```

If you specify `max_length` as a property of `db.column()`, your error goes away.

indeed, there are a couple cases where autopep8 and add-trailing-comma will fight about weirdly formatted code.

In each of these cases, there's usually a (non-automatic) refactor that makes the code more readable, for this one here's what I'd suggest:

```python
    expected_max = self.logic.calculate_shannon_entropy('01234567890123456789')
    assert self.logic.calculate_shanon_entropy('0123456789') < expected_max
```

You can find some other examples here: https://github.com/asottile/add-trailing-comma/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue+label%3Aautopep8

In this case I'd say autopep8 (and pycodestyle) is the buggier of the two -- `add-trailing-comma` is picking a much better indentation 😆 

Thanks for the issue nonetheless (I don't think there's anything to fix from `add-trailing-comma`'s side)

Hey @jplehmann, my apologies for the huge delay in responding.

The idea behind caching any addresses that weren't able to be looked up was that there are likely going to be some addresses that are not able to be geocoded; the question then becomes what to do with them? Discarding them seems wrong; an address that Google doesn't recognise is not necessarily non-existant. The answer used is to keep the address exactly as the user entered and decide what to do with it later. Addresses that aren't able to be geolocated probably need human eyes to look at them and break them into their respective components.

normally, you don't ship platform dependent compiled binaries (like the *.so file) in your source distribution, and similarly you don't store them in the git repository.

That's why I and Khaled removed it.

The tar.gz or zip source distribution only contains the python and cpp source files (and don't require cython to build the extension).

The wheel packages do contain the compiled extension module. You can rename a wheel (it's simply a zip package) and inspect its content. You'll see the pyClipper.so will be there.

You install a wheel package via `pip install -v package.whl` (-v is for verbose).

There's nothing to be concerned about.

from django.contrib import messages


class SuccessMessageMixin(object):
    """
    Adds a success message on successful form submission.
    """
    success_message = ''

    def form_valid(self, form):
        response = super(SuccessMessageMixin, self).form_valid(form)
        success_message = self.get_success_message(form.cleaned_data)
        if success_message:
            messages.success(self.request, success_message)
        return response

    def get_success_message(self, cleaned_data):
        return self.success_message % cleaned_data

@jroyal, the easiest way to do what you want to do in this library is:

```python
view_def = {
    "page_id_tester": {
        "map": "function (doc) {\n if(doc.alarm_id) {\nemit(doc.alarm_id, "
               "doc); }}",
        "reduce": "function (keys, values, rereduce) { var latest = ''; var "
                  "ts = 0; var i = 0; for(i = 0; i < values.length; i++){ "
                  "if(values[i]['timestamp'] > ts){ ts = values[i]["
                  "'timestamp']; latest = values[i];}}return latest;}",
        "dbcopy": "page-view-db"
    }
}

# assuming a connection to "database"
with DesignDocument(database, '_design/ddoc001') as ddoc:
    ddoc['views'].update(view_def)
```

This will either create or update the design document `_design/ddoc001` and either create or update the `page_id_tester` view on the server depending on whether they already exist there.

Now, I'll say that you are definitely hitting upon something that needs to be enhanced, which is the way in which we handle views.  The DesignDocument/View API that we are currently providing does appear to be limiting the content of the view.  This should/will be fixed.  But know that you can always circumvent any of these restrictions in the meantime by treating any of these objects (View, DesignDocument, Document, etc...) as a dictionary which they all do extend.

I've labeled this issue as an enhancement so we can work towards making the API a bit more inclusive and friendly.  Let me know if this works for you.

After some experiments, I've figured out the cause. information_schema.tables is a view in PostgreSQL, and PostgreSQL 9.1 probably doesn't support copying tables by "CREATE TABLE LIKE" from views.
The following sentence appears in [9.2 document](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.2/static/sql-createtable.html) for the first time, and not in [9.1](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.1/static/sql-createtable.html).

> The LIKE clause can also be used to copy columns from views, foreign tables, or composite types.

Possible solutions are:

1. specifying PostgreSQL version greater than 9.1 in .travis.yml
2. instead of a view, using a real table (such as pg_class, information_schema.sql_languages or something) as a copying source

@criccomini What version of PostgreSQL does Airflow officially support? If 9.1 is not supported, I prefer the first option for consistency with MySQL's test case.

If you've linked pyzmq against a debug build of libzmq, it will be a debug build. If pyzmq is bundling libzmq, as your path suggests, it uses Python's own compilation configuration to decide. You can tell Python to do a debug build with:

    python setup.py build -g

The releases of pyzmq are release builds, not debug builds.

C asserts cannot be handled by Python (or C) code. They unconditionally kill the process, and there is nothing that we can do about it. Any assert raised by zeromq is considered a bug in zeromq itself.

`I` think I remember getting this order before, but the key that was loaded was distorted and didn't have the correct information loaded.  I never tried loading the key from a file I was trying to transmit the public key information over HTTP.  When interpreting the bytes related to the information or a string, basically I thought the error was related to encoding issue.

I actually changed the way regular expressions were done on the key and by modifying them I was able to get the information from the key correctly determined.  It was falling to determine remove the ASCII armor from a string because it failed to interpret the ASCII armor encoded within the request.  Additionally, I changed the regular expressions for grouping the information within types.py.

`m = re.match(r"""# This capture group is optional because it will only be present in signed cleartext messages
                         (^-{5}BEGIN\ PGP\ SIGNED\ MESSAGE-{5}(?:\r?\n)
                          (Hash:\ (?P<hashes>[A-Za-z0-9\-,]+)(?:\r?\n){2})?
                          (?P<cleartext>(.*\n)+)(?:\r?\n)
                         )?
                         # armor header line; capture the variable part of the magic text
                         ^-{5}BEGIN\ PGP\ (?P<magic>[A-Z0-9 ,]+)-{5}(?:\r?\n)
                         # try to capture all the headers into one capture group
                         # if this doesn't match, m['headers'] will be None
                         ((?P<headers>(^.+:\ .+$(?:\r?\n))+))?(?:\r?\n)?
                         # capture all lines of the body, up to 76 characters long,
                         # including the newline, and the pad character(s)
                         (?P<body>([A-Za-z0-9+/]{1,75}={,2}(?:\r?\n))+)
                         # capture the armored CRC24 value
                         ^=(?P<crc>[A-Za-z0-9+/]{4})(?:\r?\n)
                         # finally, capture the armor tail line, which must match the armor header line
                         ^-{5}END\ PGP\ (?P=magic)-{5}(?:\r?\n)?
                         """,
                     text, flags=re.MULTILINE | re.VERBOSE)`

By removing the $ anchor and using re.search instead of re.match it seems to work.

Looks like this is because we only call out two specific operations in botocore where we should be automatically applying the base64 encoding: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/handlers.py#L586-L588

```
    ('before-parameter-build.ec2.RunInstances', base64_encode_user_data),
    ('before-parameter-build.autoscaling.CreateLaunchConfiguration',
     base64_encode_user_data),
```

This means that operations that have a UserData param that aren't ec2.RunInstances and autoscaling.CreateLaunchConfiguration will require users to manually base64 encode the data.

The fix is straightforward:  add the missing operations where we need to automatically base64 encode the data.  Unfortunately, this would be a backwards incompatible change for users who are already manually base64 encoding the data so we can't make this change.

I'd be interested in hearing others' ideas on this.  What about adding a config option to the clients that allows users to opt in to this behavior?

The thing I'm wondering about is why we don't just accept the pagination token as any type and perform no parsing at the Botocore level. The pagination model define the input_token and output_token. For example, from DynamoDB: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/dynamodb/2012-08-10/paginators-1.json#L10-L11. When a pagination token comes back from a service, our response parsers parse it into the appropriate Python data types because we JSON decode the response and walk it with the model. At that point, we have a valid value to pass directly into the input_token of a paginator without needing any form of AST or regex parsing because it's already been parsed out of the JSON response.

It seems like being able to accept the pagination token as a string value that is parsed into a Python type is a requirement of the CLI because it's passed in as a string argument. I'm wondering why we need to have any special handling of pagination tokens at the Botocore level.

> I stopped all supervisord my processes, obviously the one is causing it. 

I don't understand this sentence specifically, but I'm having difficulty understanding the issue description in general.  Can you please provide more details of the issue?

> I am still getting crashmail emails with DIFFERENT pid everytime.

That could be normal if a program has `autorestart=true` in `supervisord.conf`.  If a process is repeatedly exiting and being restarted, then `crashmail` will send an email for each exit, and the PID will change after each restart.

Just in case anyone else needs this in the future. You need to add this snippet to code and have your admin class derive from it.
```
class PolymorphicNestedModelAdmin(NestedModelAdmin, PolymorphicInlineSupportMixin):
    def get_inline_formsets(self, request, formsets, inline_instances,
                            obj=None, allow_nested=False):
        """Admin that fixes compatibility between nested and polymorphic admins

        Calls super on NestedModelAdmin and passes the returned list to a copy of
        PolymorphicInlineSupportMixin.get_inline_formsets()

        Both get_inline_formsets() on the parent classes need to be called in order
        for both to work instead of just one in the mro case.
        """
        inline_admin_formsets = super(NestedModelAdmin, self).get_inline_formsets(
            request, formsets, inline_instances, obj, allow_nested
        )
        # this is a copy of PolymorphicInlineSupportMixin.get_inline_formsets
        for inline_admin_formset in inline_admin_formsets:
            if isinstance(inline_admin_formset.formset, BasePolymorphicModelFormSet):
                # This is a polymorphic formset, which belongs to our inline.
                # Downcast the admin wrapper that generates the form fields.
                inline_admin_formset.__class__ = PolymorphicInlineAdminFormSet
                inline_admin_formset.request = request
                inline_admin_formset.obj = obj
        return inline_admin_formsets
```

Both `get_inline_formsets` need to be called on `NestedInlineAdminFormset` and `PolymorphicInlineSupportMixin`, due to mro, only one gets called, so adding this class works around that.

Recommendations from the upstream issue:

I think that it would be better to not depend on exact result of `re.escape`. Either use `re.escape` for calculating the part of the expected result that is out of your full control:
```
assert get_pattern(l('foo/bar')) == l(re.escape('foo/bar') + r'\Z(?ms)')
```
Or test the pattern indirectly. For example, it should match `'foo/bar'`, but not `'foo/bar\n'`.

Note that compiling the pattern `r'foo/bar\Z(?ms)'` emits a deprecation warning in 3.6+ since flags are not at the start of the expression. It is worth to rewrite `get_pattern()` until it become an error.

> I am ok with the compromise, but I would like to understand why the memory consumption went up. Could you explain @vadmeste ?

@donatello, put_object considers that the input reader is not seekable, so it allocates memory to save data to calculate hash and then send data to the server via PUT part request.

fput_object plays with file.seek(), it reads the file twice (again for hash and for PUT part) without having the obligation to save any data in the memory (syscalls and the kernel would take care of that).

Now, you would say that we save the whole data twice  in the memory.. that's true.. but it looks like the garbage collector frees data that we don't need anymore, that's what I concluded when I followed memory consumption during upload of 1Gb file.

> Why does anything have to fail? Why not solve separately for each connected component? Even if the result is a single labeled voxel, it sounds to me like this would be a correct interpretation for many inputs.

If a 0-label pixel is isolated, that means that the RW is unable to label it. An error must be triggered because not all pixels have been labelled. And if the user makes the assumption that all pixels are labelled after the random walker, he is going to have a bad surprise.

> We could check this by calling measure.label and checking that each connected components has label values both 0 and positive.

label would be a bit overkilled, as you mentioned it. A simple way to fix the first bug (error when a seed is isolated) is to add one a reflexive edge for each vertex. For the problem of isolated unlabeled voxels, I don't know if a simple solution exists.

Hi @jh0w3l05 

scikit-image needs a whole bunch of compiled binary files to work. As do e.g. pandas and SciPy. That's why PyInstaller has issues like this: 

https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/issues/3422

You probably need a fix like this:

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33001327/importerror-with-pyinstaller-and-pandas/36146649#36146649

that crawls the scikit-image path and grabs all the C compiled binaries. As an alternative, you can add hiddenimports to your PyInstaller .spec file, just like for pandas in the issues above:

```
hiddenimports = ['skimage.segmentation._slic']
```

I think this might fix your problem, but it's impossible to know. It is a PyInstaller error, not a scikit-image error.

@tisdall Yes, that makes sense.  Generally I would expect that `mypkg`’s `includeme` should be doing the scanning of `mypkg` (in which case, the `route_prefix` will “take”.)  As you note, if `mypkg` is scanned again after that, its routes will be overwritten by versions without the `route_prefix`.
When `include`ing an external package, this is not an issue, since the `scan` only finds submodules and subpackages.  Otherwise one needs to be careful about what modules are scanned — either by using `ignore=`, as you did, or by being explicit about which packages and/or modules you *do* want to scan.  I usually go the latter route, since, for me, it leads to fewer surprises.  E.g.
```
    config.include('.mysubpkg', route_prefix='/api/v1')
    config.scan('.views')
    config.scan('.subscribers')
```

Hi! I'm a maintainer of gevent and I've also contributed to this library. My organization runs a gevent-patched system that heavily uses `transaction` and I don't recall that we've run into any trouble. I also don't think we have any special code, aside from an assert in one of our modules:
```python
assert issubclass(transaction.ThreadTransactionManager, gevent.local.local)
```

This makes sure that the monkey-patching has been done soon enough. The trick, as gevent tries to document, is to make sure that monkey-patching is done *immediately* during process startup, before any other imports. In our case, we have modified `pserve` to do the patching, but that might differ in a given environment.

Our assert code is slightly more general than that to account for other thread-local things we're aware of. I can share it if that would be helpful.

#define BROTLI_BOOL unsigned char

It appears to me there are 2 facets to this capability:

1. running the 'SERVICE' subquery remotely
1. providing the results into the main query

Regarding 2. the context: `ctx` is in scope at the point of identification of a SERVICE query fragment, so this should be achievable, although I'm not sure what form is required for this as yet.

However, there is something of an implementation challenge for 1.
At this point, there is a `rdflib.plugins.sparql.parserutils.CompValue` instance, a parsed representation of the SPARQL including significant rdflib implementation details.  It is not obvious to me how to obtain the raw SPARQL query string from this.
If the raw SPARQL query string were available, a remote call to the relevant SPARQL endpoint could simply be implemented via a variety of means.

I feel that addressing this aspect is the key enabling factor for this work.

I am currently considering options including:
* passing the raw sparql fragment to the `CompValue` on creation, so that it can be retrieved and used later
* writing a `regenerate_sparql` method on `CompValue` to enable the sparql string to be regenerated on request

At present I feel like holding onto the raw fragment is less likely to introduce subtle bugs into the process.  However, CompValue is an OrderedDict, so it is not easy to alter its `__init__` parameters without changing behaviour, due to the `**values` as the last parameter to `__init__`.

Is there another way that the identified fragment of the SPARQL string that was parsed to create the `ServiceGraphPattern` `CompValue` could be in scope at the point of `evaluate`?
Could I look to subType `CompValue` to provide a `CompValueWithSparql` class that stores the SPARQL in raw form and enables it to be accessed directly within evaluate if the `ServiceGraphPattern` is identified?

https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/58d6369a12791ae7c1e259c86aeea384390d36ee/rdflib/plugins/sparql/parser.py#L930

Does parser have the raw string in scope at this point? (I'm still working my way through the logic of the parser module, I haven't grasped its functionality yet)

@mattdowle 
> why (to predict comments from community) "yet another different proprietary format from a startup that might disappear one day, and not Arrow or Parquet"

The main requirement to our format (both NFF and Jay) is that the data was stored on disk in exactly same format as it exists in memory.

As such, we cannot use Parquet, which chunks and compresses its data -- loading data from such format would involve a decompression step. (And we don't want to store compressed data in memory either, since it would slow down and complicate most algorithms).

Arrow is ideologically right for us, however the format does not (yet) have features that we absolutely require: (1) 64-bit strings, and (2) inline NA values (Arrow stores all NAs as separate bitmask arrays). In datatable we have decided since the beginning that NAs will be stored in the same format as in R's data.table, and I still believe that was the right design choice. Perhaps one day Arrow will evolve sufficiently to support our needs -- however until then it's best to have our own format. I must note that Arrow does move in the right direction generally -- for example they added string columns (`Utf8`) not too long ago, which was peeve (3) in my list.

         if am-highest-member:
             connect to 2 next-highest members (API)
             check that they are behind:
                     yes, seize master key
                     no, defer
             check that they are not masters
        if not-highest:
             connect to highest (API), check healthy:
                   if yes, defer
                   if no, remove from list, pull fresh data and resort

The not-highest chain of logic is required in case timing issues cause the server which is actually ahead to think it's not ahead, or if the ahead-most replica is actually down as well as the master.

I've had a similar experience on Kubernetes using etcd for DCS. In my case, it was DNS that stopped responding (kube-dns on the same node.) After some time, Patroni could not _find_ etcd and restarted the leader read-only to avoid a split-brain scenario.

> I've removed resource limit from API server but it did not solve issue.

It's _PostgreSQL_ that is utilizing all the resources. (CPU in my case.) The solution for me was to set the Patroni/PostgreSQL `request` to a reasonable value. This one setting affects two things:
1. The Kubernetes scheduler uses it decide on which node to place your pod. [k8s docs](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/manage-compute-resources-container/#how-pods-with-resource-requests-are-scheduled)
2. The OS uses this to decide how much time to grant each process when there's contention. The default is one full CPU (1.0 or 1000m.) [docker docs](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/run/#cpu-share-constraint)

I hadn't set a resource request, so PostgreSQL was getting something like 10x more CPU than kube-dns, delaying DNS responses long enough for requests to timeout.

(I also learned that CPU `limit` causes your process to get throttled. Even though you must set `limit` to be in the guaranteed QoS, it might not be a good idea, see kubernetes/kubernetes​#51135 for some discussion.)

What are your resource requests and limits set to on the Patroni/PostgreSQL pod?

Ok. I just run it and I manage to reproduce them successfully. 
It looks like those bytecodes are runtime bytecode (as opposed to init bytecode) and you are trying to use it in a CREATE transaction with them. Runtime bytecode will normally use CALLDATA from tx and that's empty in a CREATE tx.

So if you want to just paste the runtime bytecode into some account without running the init bytecode you could...

```
contract_account = m.create_account(code=bytecode, balance=10000000)
```

The error messages are bad. And CALLDATA should be empty there but not None in your examples. I'll fix those soon. Thanks.

Hrm. This is now extremely problematic. Try `openssl s_client -connect "host:port" -CApath /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/certifi/weak.pem`

Probably because NTLM authentication relies on receiving 401 responses. It works like

* Send un authenticated request
* Receive 401 response with header that lists supported auth mechanisms
* Send NTLM negotiate message
* Receive 401 response with NTLM challenge message
* Send NTLM authenticate message
* Receive 200 response as user is now authenticated.

I don't know how the Retry operations change the behaviour but it could be that it adds a hook to 401 responses and continues to try and send the same message (retry) without any of the NTLM messages.

FYI, your test:

    $ PYTHONPATH=lib/ python -c 'import gcloud.datastore'

Is not valid. It's not enough to set `PYTHONPATH`. `lib` is actually a site-directory, and `ext.vendor` explicitly handles processing it as a site-directory. Namespace packages (such as `google`) won't work at all unless processed in a site-directory.

For completeness:

    $ python -c 'import site; site.addsitedir("lib"); import google.protobuf; print(google.protobuf)'
    <module 'google.protobuf' from '/private/tmp/1624/lib/google/protobuf/__init__.pyc'>

@Bogdanp Sorry this has been happening. gRPC support for datastore [was added in 0.19.0][1], so the 0.20.0 upgrade wouldn't have changed anything. `UNAVAILABLE` is essentially the [same as a 503][2] (but we expect [a retry is just fine][3]). Why is that relevant? Because a 503 is a sign that something bad happened in the server, not that something was wrong with the request.

So your issue is essentially two issues:

- Fix #2497 
- Add support for retries

Which is why you have the break.  So, TBH, I missed that impact of moving `dll.dill` to `dill._dill` on storing the name of the functions inside of the internal module.  We don't test that new pickles will load into old versions.  Sorry about that.

Ok, the first problem, the BlockDevice problem, is a misunderstanding of Cloudformation - `BlockDeviceMapping` is a Property, not a Resource.  That means it's meant to be the value of an attribute of a Cloudformation Resource, not added to the Template directly itself (ie: with `add_resource`).

You want to add a `BlockDeviceMapping` to the `BlockDeviceMappings` property of the EC2 Instance you are launching:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-ec2-instance.html#cfn-ec2-instance-blockdevicemappings

You can see an example here: https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/2b149fd29e3264506e7ffa2781bc207ed9ed440a/examples/EC2_Remove_Ephemeral_Drive.py#L39

Sorry - I missed your followup. For the record - this does not make anything new public - there is no change to the API, it just makes calling it safer (imo).

A task writer that calls `vt.create_results_dirs` (which is a reasonable thing to do - there is no alternative namespace for a task to do work, caching or no) and does not set `cache_target_dirs` might have the following code:

```
with self.invalidated(targets) as invalidation_check:
  for vt in invalidation_check.all_vts:
    vt.create_results_dirs(self.workdir)
    if not vt.valid:
      <do work>
    self.context.products.get('my_product').add(vt.target, vt.results_dir)
```

On master that will work - but only as long as the vt is invalid. Once it becomes valid, the `create_results_dirs` call noops and the `vt.results_dir` reference on the last line will raises the following exception:

        ValueError('No results_dir was created for {}'.format(self))`

Using the `create_target_dirs` instead of `cache_target_dirs` *does* allow it to work. But it wasn't clear to me that was its intention - and I know this code well. 

In my mind that task developer already signaled their intention to create the target dirs, calling the  public `create_results_dirs` method. There is no documentation that clearly says you should prefer to use the property over that method so it seemed reasonable to try and ensure the task worked for either, especially since it seems to come at no cost to the rest of the world.

This is part of an overall philosophical push (that I'm putting out there for feedback) that Django settings files should primarily be used for Django settings, not as a place for us to funnel all our config options through. Throughout Kolibri, we can directly reference the standardized (and validated) config options via `kolibri.utils.conf.OPTIONS`.

An additional thing I'm thinking could be good:

- Right now we hardcode the `SECRET_KEY` into the base Django settings, and since most users won't be specifying a custom settings module and overriding this value, it may be safest for us to add an ini option for this, and write a random value for the option to the ini file (if it doesn't have one defined in there already).

It's not immediately clear from the official documentation that additional configuration of [aldryn-boilerplates](https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-boilerplates#configuration) is needed in order for the installation to work. It is mentioned in the docs and a [link](https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-boilerplates#configuration) is provided, but the mention is so brief that it's easily overlooked.

Perhaps it would be a good idea to include the following text snippet along with the link:

# Configuration

## Django 1.5 - 1.7

````
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    ...
    'aldryn_boilerplates',
    ...
]

TEMPLATE_CONTEXT_PROCESSORS = [
    ...
    'aldryn_boilerplates.context_processors.boilerplate',
]

STATICFILES_FINDERS = [
    'django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.FileSystemFinder',
    # important! place right before django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder
    'aldryn_boilerplates.staticfile_finders.AppDirectoriesFinder',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder',
]

TEMPLATE_LOADERS = [
    'django.template.loaders.filesystem.Loader',
    # important! place right before django.template.loaders.app_directories.Loader
    'aldryn_boilerplates.template_loaders.AppDirectoriesLoader',
    'django.template.loaders.app_directories.Loader',
]
````
Now set the name of the Boilerplate you want to use in your project:

````
ALDRYN_BOILERPLATE_NAME = 'bootstrap3'
````

## Django 1.8+

In general configuration stays the same but you should respect changes that were introduced by django 1.8. In particular in Django 1.8 context processors were moved from django.core to django.template.

Be sure to include aldryn_boilerplates to INSTALLED_APPS, adjust STATICFILES_FINDERS and finally configure TEMPLATES.

For TEMPLATES you need to add aldryn_boilerplates.context_processors.boilerplate to context_processors and alter loaders in the same way as we do it for Django versions prior to 1.8.

Note that in the example below we are altering the default values, so if you are using something that is custom - don't forget to add that too.

Here is an example of a simple configuration:

````
INSTALLED_APPS = [

Hi Jakub,
Are you using AMG as a preconditioner? In this case, the preconditioned
residual that is used in the Krylov method is computed and saved. In most
cases this should be a good measure of residual reduction. One could also
compute the actual residual each iteration, but this is an additional matvec
that doesn't contribute to the actual solve. Note that I have experienced
situations (mostly in developing nonsymmetric methods) where I end up
with a singular preconditioner for nonsymmetric problems, and the algorithm
stops because residual went from 10^50 to 10^40... Perhaps there is a check
we should add for situations like this? Or check the actual residual before
stopping?
-ben

The team and I reviewed this and had a design discussion on how best to approach predefined objects, since they are unique among all objects, they need some special handling.  You're pull request is a very good start.  Here are some design factors we considered when figuring out the best way to handle this.

1.  The Predefined class should not inherit from VersionedPanObject.  Although this approach is advantageous in many ways, it results in many methods that no longer apply and inconsistent behavior.  So we think it would be better to inherit from `object` instead and just create the methods that are needed.

2.  The Predefined class can be pretty simple.  It just needs to have some dictionaries (you've created these already) and some methods to populate those dictionaries.  I imagine methods like the following:

    def refresh_applications(self, names=()):
    def refresh_services(self, names=()):
    etc...

3.  Examples of how to refresh and access predefined objects might look something like this:

```
# Refresh two predefined applications from the firewall
fw.predefined.refresh_applications(['gotomeeting', 'zoom'])
# Collect application objects into a list
application_list = fw.predefined.applications(['gotomeeting', 'zoom'])
# Or get them one at a time
app = fw.predefined.application('gotomeeting')
```
This approach fits very well with linking references (issue #23) because references can be dynamically refreshed as needed preventing a massive transfer of the entire predefined object definition.

I don't have a systematic way of doing it. In general, you have to match the wavelength of the signal to the distances between the microphones in your array. If the wavelength is very long compared to the inter-mic separation, then there is barely any phase difference between microphones and localization will be poor, as you can observe on the bottom parts of the graphs. On the contrary, if the wavelength is much shorter than the distance, you start to get aliasing, i.e. there is a phase ambiguity. Your target is the sweet spot between the two regions.

I believe that `ttfautohint`’s `--composites` option should — at least in theory — work fine with transformed components, because it disables TT instructions inherited from the parent glyphs and applies instructions on the outlines of the composite glyphs. When Werner originally developed this, it was the only, default behavior of `ttfautohint`. Back then, I asked him to make this behavior optional because the separate instructing of all composite glyphs significantly increased the file size, potentially offering little benefit in many fonts. So now by default, if you don’t use `--composites`, `ttfautohint` will not apply any hinting to the composite glyphs, relying solely on the hinting inherited from the parent glyphs. 

But the case of transformed (I mean transformed in a way other than shifting, of course) components is an interesting one: instructions inherited from the parent glyphs will most likely be useless in that case. It would be useful if Werner added an option, which does perform full hinting on glyphs which have transformed components, but not do the separate hinting on untransformed components. That should actually be the default behavior, because that would offer the best balance between effectiveness and size.

With a "real" decorator, it could be the decorator itself which is responsible for populating a global list *or* via "marking" the function w/ an attribute. e.g.:

```
_public_api = []
def public(obj):
    _public_api.append(obj)
    return obj
```
Versus:
```
def public(obj):
    obj._public_api = True
    return obj
```

Perhaps there's a reason to prefer one over the other; in either case it's easier for the hook-method to figure out what to do (i.e. ``getattr(obj, '_public_api', False)`` versus ``obj in _global_api`` basically).

8 years old bug.

The background is: to add a Twisted Web resource that runs WebSocket, we (Autobahn) have to get at the full initial HTTP request bytes - which have been eaten away by Twisted Web before. 

I consider the code in Autobahn a "gentle hack" .. due to lack of appropriate interfaces in Twisted (and me, and that time, not having the patience to go through months of discussions to land something on Twisted;)

As to the question of "how much performance impact is `.debug()` not being a no-op?" I *think* the answer will be "significant" .. but I don't have hard figures for that. The reason I think this is that all internal logging is premised on the fact of it being no-op'd out if that log-level isn't turned on, and `.debug()` is not something you'd turn on in a production router for long periods of time.

(The amount of time "interpolating" strings -- that is, string-ifying and inserting variables into the format strings -- is non-trivial, and some of the debug logging is pretty low-level). That said, some hard figures would be *very nice* to have.

On the premise that most people will NOT want debugging messages in their logs, I think "by default it's off" is fine. If you *did* expect and want debug messages in the logs, you have to call `start_logging(...)` at some point .. but to use logging "properly" with Autobahn Python you have to do that anyway. Also, this WILL get called for you if you use the recommended, high-level `run()` API -- it only won't get called if you're opting in to calling `start()` on your components yourself (at which point, you should be arranging for `txaio.start_logging()` to be called precisely once anyway). Perhaps "this needs better documentation" is the real solution...?

To proactively address questions that will probably arise, the config package was a significant pain point prior to this refactor.  It contained a model for configuration data, an interface for (potentially) multiple implementations of a config backend, and one implementation of that based on etcd.  Having everything in one package like that made it tricky to code to the interface, agnostic of the implementation and made it difficult to create alternate implementations (e.g. an in-memory implementation intended to aid in testing).  The refactor was aimed at breaking that package up cleanly to allow better reuse and testing.

The client requires you to authenticate to the controller, so that limits the client to using one cluster as a time.  To switch, you log out of one and into the next.  That's definitely inconvenient if you want to push the same app to four clusters.  This is not to mention you'd also have to juggle multiple git remotes for the pushes.

What I'd recommend is using some CI server like Jenkins to accomplish that instead.  The deployment workfliw becomes one where you merge into some branch that Jenkins watches to kick off a deploy.  That could optionally trigger a test suite first and then a chain of jobs, where each one deploys the app to a specific region by logging in to the correct cluster and pushing to the correct remote.  You get a lot of other benefits from that workflow as well, including notifications when deployments are triggered, completed, or failed.

So that doesn't have `blas[track_features=blas_openblas]` and also, on Windows, `python[track_features=vc*]` anymore, which should cover the biggest chunk.
`openjdk[track_features=openjdk]` is another thing we have to get rid of -- not just on `conda-forge`, but also for `defaults::openjdk=8.0.152`.

I guess having `track_features=vc*` on the `vc` packages (apart from the newest/preferred one, i.e., `vc=14.1`) could make sense to prevent `vc` runtime switches for an environment. Though, I'm definitely not sold on this one as I'd rather avoid `(track_)features` stuff wherever possible. But I haven't given that special `vc[track_features=vc*]` case much thought yet..

>  think, there could be some people who would be depending on user_site packages in a conda environment. So, a solution is to have an argument like "--no-user-site" in the conda create command, which then doesn't include user_site packages. 

The problem is interfacing this conda environment parameter to Python itself which is what is in control of whether `~/.local` is added to the `sys.path`. Using an env. var is sub-optimal for external tooling that does not use the correct `conda activate` procedure. I *guess* we could put this in a file in the root of the environment and then modify our python executable to always look for this? Changing the behaviour of the `python` executable isn't something to do lightly though!
