ok, I took a debugger and this is what I found on my setup after some digging around:

in `conda/core/repodata.py` around line 85 (`SubdirData.query_all`):
```
            from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
            executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(10)
            futures = (executor.submit(
                SubdirData(Channel(url)).query, package_ref_or_match_spec
            ) for url in channel_urls)
            return tuple(concat(future.result() for future in as_completed(futures)))
```

Note that `futures` here is a generator expression. 
On my setup, `concurrent.futures.as_completed(fs)` assumes that `fs` is a sequence that can be iterated over multiple times, which does not work with a generator expression obviously.

`concurrent.futures` is since  version 3.0.0 (https://github.com/agronholm/pythonfutures/commit/f3d2563b4345b108a6197a36411aec19064ee15f)   is more flexible about this, but apparently my `conda` uses version 2.2.0 (I think, not completely sure about this)

The hack that fixes the situation for me is as follows.
In `${anaconda_install_dir}/lib/python2.7/site-packages/concurrent/futures/_base.py`, I updated (based on https://github.com/agronholm/pythonfutures/commit/f3d2563b4345b108a6197a36411aec19064ee15f) the `as_completed` function around line 200 so that `fs` is a set that can be iterated over multiple times:
```
def as_completed(fs, timeout=None):
    # ...

    fs = set(fs)
    with _AcquireFutures(fs):
        # ...
```


Now, `conda search pandas` and `conda create` work again for me as expected

Forcing conda-env to the lower version prevents it from being automatically updated.  When that happens, it is trying to run the post-link script.  The post link script is broken for Python 2.7.  If you don't run the post-link script (by deleting it, and preventing it from getting installed), then yes, I think my suggestion solves it all.
For more background, it happens because old versions use an http handler that doesn't follow redirects.  You can also change the configuration to use all https:// urls.

I guess I never noticed this before because I always define my envs_dirs, but the second is, imo, a design flaw.

```
return '~/.conda/envs', '~/envs', join(self.root_dir, 'envs')
```

Should be

```
return '~/.conda/envs', '~/envs'
```

Here's what I think is happening.

With `conda install git`: conda first maximizes the version, build, and timestamp of `git`, and obtains `2.14.1 h8288424_2`. This introduces a `vc 9.*` dependency, which in turn introduces the `vc9` feature. Conda then determines that it can do without `vc9` _and_ `vc14` by downgrading Python. 

I'd say the primary problem here is that `git` should not be depending on vc9. I am _sure_ I have said this, multiple times: a package should _never_ include a `track_feature` package as a _dependency_. In this _particular_ case, `git` should depend on `vs2008_runtime` directly. But packages that really _need_ the `vc9` feature should specify it as a _feature_, not a dependency.

This is an import error with pkg_resources, which is part of setuptools.  No idea why it's happening, but #5496 is a direct fix for conda.  It'll be included in 4.3.22.

In the meantime, it's possible `pip install -U setuptools` will get you out of this hole.

where the inner expression is (1+1). After calling self.process on them, we get:

```
['-', '2', '*', '1']
```

which self.neg leaves untouched (it doesn't actually do the negation). It seems like the method is expecting some more like this, which is what happens when the input is `-2*1` instead of `-(1+1)*1`:

```
['-2', ' ', '*', '1']
```

Seems to be solves with commit 9400112034479bad7a217fbe5b920cbad90db2d .
It was actually a duplicate of #61 .

@LarsAsplund, @kraigher 

I am working now on making Vunit understand my errors suring the clean-up phase. The way I see it, errors are of 3 categories:

1. UVM_ERROR - messages that are generated and tracked by the UVM base. These can be 'fetched' during the clean-up phase quite easily to make Vunit register a FAIL
2. Internal errors - for example, a null pointer, memory allocation which causes the simulation to stop with a tool-specific message. In this case Vunit recognizes it as a FAIL since its clean-up phase was not called.
3. untraceable functional erros - these include error messages that do not cause the simulation to stop, and cannot be 'fetched' within the SystemVerilog code. This includes errors generated by third-party IPs/blackboxes, VIPs or any HDL code that uses a $error system task.

It seems that I can make Vunit's PASS/FAIL recognize category 3 only by parsing the transcript. Meaning, I have to do this in the Python-world. Do you have an example where this is done ?

I see that the file test_output/<Some long name+HASH>/output.txt is the name assigned to the simulation log. I am looking for a way to access this from the Python-script. Is there some vendor-independent way I can get the pointer to this file within the Python-world ?

Ok, I think I understand what is going on here:

1. warnings in vunit get printed to stderr, even if the vunit process returns 0.  That's probably ok.
2. tcl command pipelines "generate errors exactly as described for exec" according to [here](http://wiki.tcl.tk/1241) but unlike exec, there's no --ignorestderr option so if anything is printed to stderr, the close of the pipeline will return with an error code which is what's going on here.

I also found a possible solution:
I copied the vunit_compile procedure into my own tcl file and changed the python subprocess.call() method to put stderr=sys.stdout instead of sys.stderr.

After making that change, the compilation is successful and restart works as expected.  Introducing a syntax error to a file will still make the subprocess call return non-zero and the compile is properly detected by tcl as a failure.

@krishnan-gopal I am not against using plusargs instead of generics. In our current System Verilog test benches the parameter is a private implementation detail so it could be changed without any test bench modifications. The reason we used a parameter was that it would work the same as in VHDL and parts of the Python code can be unaware if it is a VHDL or Verilog test bench since the same mechanism is used. If we use plusargs we need to introduce a special handling of VHDL and Verilog test benches.

The problem with the 'works but "fails"' is that Curator cannot extrapolate the correct destination index name from Elasticsearch date math.  That's why the one which is statically named succeeds and the others give a failure message.

If you're still looking for use cases, there are three that come to mind immediately for me:

1.  Reindex daily data into weekly or monthly indices after then end of the week / month. There are two clusters I'm working on right now that would benefit from functionality like this. The pattern in both cases would be combining daily indices into either weekly or monthly, once the week/month is over. 
2. Reduce shard count after index becomes immutable. This use case would probably be replaced by https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/5.0/indices-shrink-index.html. But then again the cases where I've done this have always had to do with starting with a higher shard count to parallelize writes and then shrinking the shard count back down once the index is immutable. Other words, the first use case is much more generic and powerful than this use case. 
3. Reindexing old data to account for schema incompatibilities introduced by dynamic mappings and/or schema evolution. It is not uncommon to see log data evolve as users gain more experience with Kibana / ES. A great example that came to mind recently was status code in Nginx logs. Initially IP addresses were indexed as strings, before the devs realized they could be indexed as IP. We changed this to an IPv4 mapping in the index template. But, in doing so, we also introduced a backwards incompatibility in the mappings for the alias. The conflict itself can be avoided by never changing field names -- but a lot of times it's important to be able to retroactively fix some mapping in log data, and that usually involves ad hoc scripts either scanning and scrolling or using the _reindex API.

I don't do anything special in my dev environment when editing code. Even though pip installs a copy of the OpenAddresses code into the venv, that copy will be ignored if it can find one in the local directory thanks to `''` at the front of `sys.path`:

>>> import openaddr

<module 'openaddr' from 'openaddr/__init__.pyc'>

Your paper
https://animation.rwth-aachen.de/media/papers/2016-MIG-StableRotation.pdf
was used to implement finding the rotation to diagonalize an inertia tensor.

I'm finding that there is a problem with this, when you use a symmetric
matrix with the algorithm you presented no solution can be found. Inertia
tensors are always symmetric.

For example try:

0.00076275, -0.00047298, 0
-0.00047298, 0.0019185, 0
0, 0, 0.0022293


Trying various starting q's as suggested in the paper always converges to
q=0. However, SVD finds the solution.

You can see the discussion unfolding here:
https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/issues/1667

Best regards,

@blink1073 that's definitely possible and would be more robust. To do that, we would need to maintain a cache of messages on *all* channels all the time, in case of dropped connections in the future, rather than only when connections are lost. Doing that points to a different mechanism, because it makes it *inevitable* that the cache gets huge, rather than possible. To do that, we need to spill to disk with an efficient culling mechanism. To me, that sounds like a job for sqlite.

If I were doing that, I would make last_msg_id part of the websocket connect request to trigger the replay.

The thought came up because in my client, I am storing callbacks for each executed cell, based on the parent header; if a message comes in from the kernel with a "parent" indicating a given cell, it will be handled with the appropriate callback. Unless it's defined when more messages cannot be sent, though, the callbacks can never be freed and the client is guaranteed to use up progressively more resources, since it never knows that it will not receive any more messages.

If that is the intended / expected behavior for clients for the time being, go ahead and close this :)

Yeah, but I think putting numpy into setup_requires can mean it gets
installed via easy_install, which IIRC can mean a non-optimal version of
numpy getting installed, and may need to be compiled too, whereas pip will
bring down pre-built wheels these days. Also means that some shenanigans
need to be added to the setup.py script so that defining the cython
extensions is deferred until after setup_requires dependencies have been
obtained, which adds some complexity to the setup.py script. Things may
have moved on since I last looked into this so happy to consider a PR if
there's a good solution. But FWIW if you're using pip/venv I think it's
just easier to install numpy first, then install scikit-allel.

``shed_lint`` first finds "tool shed repositories" (e.g. directories with ``.shed.yml`` files) and then lints "any included tool" (right now that just means everything other the ``.shed.yml`` file but in the future it will be smarter and only lint things that would be uploaded). ``lint --recurisive`` will just lint all tools - it doesn't need to find a ``.shed.yml`` file first. I guess ``shed_lint`` will assume the directory you give it corresponds to a tool shed repository if it cannot find any ``.shed.yml`` files - so perhaps in your case they look pretty simillair. There is also differences related to the way they are displayed - ``shed_lint`` tries to show tool linting in the context of the repository with a little ``+`` it is slight. The other big difference is that ``shed_lint`` lint tool shed artifacts as well.

But yeah - ``shed_lint`` should also allow multiple inputs.

Catching CalledProcessError is harmful and needs to be removed. Catching OSError may improve usability, but only if errno is checked for ENOENT or whatever errno is set to in case `docker` can't be found on PATH. This is to address issue #96

> I did not get pytest-selenium support version with Firefox. I used Firefox 45.0.2, I did not think only have to use Firefox 48.

GeckoDriver and Marionette support Firefox 48 and onwards. See https://github.com/mozilla/geckodriver#supported-firefoxen for more details.

> And besides, the GeckoDriver is located on /usr/bin

Your exception is "The path to the driver executable must be set by the webdriver.gecko.driver". Have you tried doing this on your remote node? Note that geckodriver must be on the path on your remote node, not your host (unless they are the same).

> pytest-selenium not have clear instructions or command how to use with GeckoDriver

I'll take care of updating the documentation soon (unless someone submits a pull request before I get to it). This will be very similar to the Google Chrome or Internet Explorer documentation that simply states that for Firefox 48 you will need to download the GeckoDriver binary and make sure it is in the path.

> --driver Remote --capability browserName firefox  --capability marionette true --driver-path=/usr/bin/geckodriver

This is clearly not your full command line, however from this I see that you're using a remote WebDriver instance but not specifying a host or port. This means pytest-selenium will be expecting a Selenium server instance running locally. Specifying the marionette capability is fine, although in 3.0beta this is not needed. The driver path looks good assuming this is the correct path to the GeckoDriver binary. Also please check that the file is executable.

If you're still having issues please provide your full command line and stack trace.

After some thinking on the subject, i think the best approach is to follow naming pattern derived from json kind. This would mean as follows:

1. If the the last part of json kind for a name of a resource is in plural, the sub-collection class name of that resource would have '_s' added.  Then subsequently, the resource class name would be in plural. For example:
````
tm:security:firewall:rule-list:rules:rulesstate

Collection = RULES_S
Resource = RULES
````
2. If the the last part of json kind for a name of a resource is in singular, the sub-collection class name of that resource would have 's' or '_s' added. However name of the class for resource would be in singular. For example:
````
tm:security:firewall:address-list:address-liststate

Collection = Address_Lists
Resource = Address_List

tm:asm:policies:policystate

Collection = Policies_s
Resource = Policy
`````

The binary was chosen to avoid issues with Apache's parsing order.

Apache only makes  a single pass through the configuration so the ordering of includes like
```Include conf/*```
are problematic.  The parser's representation has to match Apache's.  (Variables can be defined and then depended later on in the configuration.  More problematic for IfDefine scenarios)

The binary seemed like the easiest way to precisely get which variables Apache knew about.

(Although somewhere in the tree there is an implementation using configuration directives.)

Best,

Earlier today I tried the acme challenge and again failed.

However, I noticed that the error message prompted by the client was changed (did some re-formatting to suit the convenience of reading):

```
Failed authorization procedure.
example.cn (http-01):
urn:acme:error:connection
:: The server could not connect to the client to verify the domain
:: DNS query timed out during CAA-record lookup of example.cn

IMPORTANT NOTES:
 - The following 'urn:acme:error:connection' errors were reported by
   the server:

   Domains: example.cn
   Error: The server could not connect to the client to verify the
   domain
```

Then I switched one of my domains' authoritative name server to DNSPod and, after waiting for some minutes (to wait the cache really being flushed), I tried again and succeeded.

Given that, I think

1.  The boulder team has cleared up their error message by specifically pointing out that the failure is due to the CAA-record lookup issue.
2.  As the DNSPod's employee stated out in [this thread](https://www.v2ex.com/t/241819), they have made DNSPod compatible with LE mainly by adding the (optional, as described in [RFC 6844](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6844)) CAA-record lookup support.

    So, for those who still suffering the "DNS query timeout" issue, a possibly workaround is switching their resolver to a service provider which support CAA-record lookup (like DNSPod).
    
    Further more, if LE (or the acme challenge) has no plan to remove the dependency of CAA-record verification procedure, we can close this issue now.

This is a major issue. There must be some ```-renew-only-if-missing``` option for automation. The current behavior to ask question like "Do you want to (R)eplace or (C)ancel" will ruin your automated deployments. Specifying ```--renew-by-default``` will easily lead to this issue, even with modest number of (successful runs) wile testing your automated scripts.

I can't run the `*-oldest` tests on my system either, however, we specifically want to test against `PyOpenSSL 0.13` as that is the latest version available in some of the distributions where we are packaged. The tests run correctly in Travis though.

Perhaps we could just add a comment about this in the developer documentation.
Ready for rereview.

[branch: ankostis/pypiserver@proxy_rpc](/ankostis/pypiserver/tree/proxy_rpc)

Pyhon's XML-RPC support across version is very unstable.  This fact, combined with PyPi-server's well-known network problems, makes the time required to ensure a stable and tested implementation disproportionately high compared to the benefits from utilizing an http-proxy when running the ``-U`` command. 

I note that this *check-for-updates-on-all-uploaded-packages* command is not necessary for the core service offered by this project, and any dependencies (i.e. the `requests` library) would make sense if more XML-RPC PyPi-API functionality is planned (which is not).

That is what happens now. The check is still present [here](https://github.com/bartaelterman/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L766). However, I think what we really want is (in pseudo code):

```
if value is in DATETIME_DESERIALIZATION_FUNCS:
    parse with that function
elif value is a valid strptime time formatting string:
    parse with strptime
else
    parse with dateutil
```

However:

1. From the API perspective, I think that results in weird behaviour. Either the user sets the `format` parameter to `rfc` or `iso` (which will result in parsing with the function in `DATETIME_DESERIALIZATION_FUNCS`), or he gives a time formatting string, which will result in parsing with `strptime`. If the parameter is not set, `iso` is used as default. So from a user perspective, to what value should I set the `format` parameter to have dateutil do the parsing? Some random string not being `iso`, `rfc` or a time formatting string and not `None`? Maybe the best option would then be to override the behaviour in case the parameter is `None`.
2. I'm not sure how to check whether the given string is a valid strptime formatting string anyway.

So I agree that parsing with dateutil could be useful, but we should give it a proper place in the API.

        cnn = Convolution2D(16, 3, 3, border_mode="same", activation='relu')(x)
        cnn = MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2))(cnn)

        cnn = Convolution2D(32, 3, 3, border_mode="same", activation='relu')(cnn)
        cnn = MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2))(cnn)

        cnn = Convolution2D(64, 3, 3, border_mode="same", activation='relu')(cnn)
        cnn = MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2))(cnn)

        cnn = Convolution2D(128, 3, 3, border_mode="same", activation='relu')(cnn)
        cnn = MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2, 2))(cnn)

        cnn = Flatten()(cnn)

        nets.append(cnn)
        inputs.append(x)

merged = concatenate(nets)
merged = Reshape((length, 25088))(merged)
out = LSTM(256, name='lstm')(merged)
out = Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')(out)
t = Model(inputs, out)
```

Can you test with the version on pip and a simplified test file that only tests the code snippet given on this ticket? The versions of the module on pip should correlate 1:1 with the "[Prepare vX.X.X for release](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript-python/commit/ac9d56b19e550ba68e857d4aedb96c654da91cfc)" commits from GitHub, as I make those commits just before publishing to PyPI.

For example, use a script like the `example.py` from the root of this repo and test the bare minimum RiveScript code in a terminal session to see whether it's a bug in RiveScript itself or a bug in your specific application.

-----

For more background and ideas of where the problem might lie:

* If you have more RiveScript code in your actual bot than what you pasted on this ticket, it could be uncovering a different problem than this ticket suggests.

  I've had bug reports in the past where the sorting algorithm for `%Previous` was incorrect, and the bug would appear when you have multiple triggers that all share a matching `%Previous` command. The root cause was that the triggers weren't being sorted properly and one of them was made un-matchable whereas the other one would work.
* I've seen people lately using RiveScript for Facebook Messenger bots and similar platforms, where they built a web server (such as with Flask) to provide the webhook for the bot platform.

  That architecture introduces all sorts of new moving parts that aren't present in more simple, single process command line applications. For example, a common deployment mode for Flask apps is to use gunicorn and run multiple instances of the app, for example, have four different Python processes all running the app and load balancing requests. This is generally good for simple stateless web apps, but RiveScript's automatic in-memory caching of user variables becomes a problem, because different Python processes might handle different requests from the same user, and they won't all agree on the user variables. Consider using something like [rivescript-redis](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript-python/tree/master/contrib/redis) if this sounds familiar. [More info on this kind of thing is in the RiveScript Wiki.](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript/wiki/Make-a-Single-Shared-Bot-Instance)

To generalize, `%Previous` only works when the bot is able to remember the previous reply given to the user, and for that to work, user variables have to be kept around somehow between requests. If the user is hitting a different process with each request, or if the RiveScript instance is being torn down and rebuilt on each request, then their variables won't be consistent and their last reply will be missing or incorrect.

> I don't really understand why separating them would seem clearer.

Just conceptually. I think it's easier to understand "this layer has some parameters (`params`), and then it has tags for these parameters (`param_tags`)" than "this layer has some parameters, and they also have tags, but we just call all that `params` anyway".

> are there any concrete advantages to separating them?

Technically not so many, no. It's easier to collect all the parameters when you're not filtering them (you can just collect all `Layer.params` lists instead of `Layer.params.keys()`, the latter of which has to copy the doubly linked list into a list in case of Python 2). But there's no crucial technical advantage, just the conceptual one.

@benjaoming Here's my perspective: it's not about whether it's useful at all, but whether it's useful for one's particular use case. This is when it is helpful to have a demo / video: when you're exploring lots of projects and looking for one with particular looks/feature set, basically one that fits your project the most, it's good if the maintainer puts some teaser on the website. Also, lots of people are browsing sites like hacker news and stumbling upon this kind of projects and many times when you stumble upon it randomly, it's easier to remember a screenshot than a name - it's just another way of amplifying memory and giving enough information to subconsciousness to have it work for you as well.

Also, if you put it somewhere high in docs/readme, it personally gives me the "this is easy" vibe - I just saw the screenshot, maybe also a small snippet that shows how easy it is to integrate the thing, it makes it more likely that I come back and choose the project as the first one to try even if it didn't have most stars in its category.

Here's another reason: at one point you might just decide not to maintain the project anymore because of some unforeseeable reasons, perhaps even without marking the project as "not in development anymore". From this point on, I'd expect any project to start to decay, losing compatibility with whatever it's attached to and researching "what does it do exactly" is more complicated because you might need to port it or solve some installation problems.

Hello,

> Is it possible to get the value of t41 directly (or something like offset + computed_address) ?

You can get the value of a temporary register from the `temps` attribute of `SimStateScratch` plugin. This could be done _after_ stepping a state forward by inspecting the `state.scratch.temps` of the desired successor.

> Also, is there a (simple?) way to know which memory addresses had been read/write between two sm.step()?

This could be done by setting a breakpoints on `'mem_read'` and `'mem_write'` events using the `state.inspect.make_breakpoint` or `state.inspect.add_breakpoint` methods, whichever you prefer. When the breakpoint is triggered, the values you're looking for could be obtained from the `state.inspect.mem_read_address` and `state.inspect.mem_write_address` respectively.

```python
def handle_wechat_request():
    """
    处理回复微信请求
    """
    signature = request.args.get('signature', '')
    timestamp = request.args.get('timestamp', '')
    nonce = request.args.get('nonce', '')
    encrypt_type = request.args.get('encrypt_type', 'raw')
    msg_signature = request.args.get('msg_signature', '')
    try:
        check_signature(app.config['WECHAT']['OPEN_PLATFORM']['TOKEN'],
                        signature, timestamp, nonce)
    except InvalidSignatureException:
        return redirect(app.config['SERVER_URL'])
    if request.method == 'GET':
        echo_str = request.args.get('echostr', '')
        return echo_str
    # POST request
    if encrypt_type == 'raw':
        abort(404)
    else:
        # encryption mode
        try:
            client = get_open_platform_client()
            xml = client.crypto.decrypt_message(request.data, msg_signature,
                                                timestamp, nonce)
            msg = xmltodict.parse(to_text(xml))['xml']
            info_type = msg['InfoType']
            if info_type == "component_verify_ticket":
                data = client.cache_component_verify_ticket(request.data, msg_signature,
                                                            timestamp,
                                                            nonce)
                print(data)
            elif info_type == "authorized":
                print(msg)
            elif info_type == "unauthorized":
                data = client.get_unauthorized(request.data, msg_signature,
                                               timestamp, nonce)
                print(data)
        except (InvalidSignatureException, InvalidAppIdException):
            abort(403)
        else:
            return "success", 200
```

Sorry I wasn't able to look at this for so long!

- I haven't had a chance to look at the memory issue you encountered. I'm glad you found a workaround. Maybe that's good enough, or maybe we should dig deeper?
- The tests look very thorough. Testing the definitions in isolation is a great idea.
- Adding a new kind of request means adding a new test to the swagger tests. Maybe it would be cool if there were a way to avoid this? For example, maybe we could have a test that just makes every request possible. Or is it at least possible to verify that all requests in the spec were exercised by the test suite, and fail if one wasn't? (This might not work for error responses, which I think we still have to write out explicitly.)
- If it makes sense to use `cornice_swagger`, then go ahead, but if it doesn't suit our use cases, then I don't want to spend a lot of time trying to force it to fit. In our meeting today, you mentioned that our Colander schemae are actually shared, so probably not a good match for a user-facing schema. In addition, some of our API involves custom headers, which I bet aren't captured in any Colander schema. Both of these seem like good reasons why `cornice_swagger` wouldn't fit.
- @gabisurita proposed making Swagger stuff into a plugin. @Natim didn't have any objections. @leplatrem ? We agreed that we should proceed as-is (i.e. in this repo) for now, since moving it should be easy once it exists. I think it should stay in this repo as long as we're maintaining it manually.
- @gabisurita asked where the Swagger view should live (`kinto` or `kinto.core`). I think that as long as it's being maintained manually, it should live in `kinto`, given that the views that are documented are there too. If we come up with some way of generating it automatically, it could live in `kinto.core` so that projects "inheriting" from `kinto.core` could get Swagger support too. What do you think @Natim @leplatrem ?

Custom headers like If-Match and If-Not-Match weren't there when I looked at this earlier, but I see that they're now. In a meeting today, @gabisurita said that support for headers has just landed in Swagger 2.0.

>> 1. How should responses be documented?
>>
> I think currently cornice does not support that @leplatrem ? Maybe this can be added as docstrings and extracted from there?
>

Indeed, there's nothing in Cornice right now. We could add extra attributes on Cornice services to specify the responses schemas.

As you noted already, in Kinto we still have some custom validation that should be moved to Colander schemas (see #873 #880).

> If it makes sense to use cornice_swagger, then go ahead, but if it doesn't suit our use cases, then I don't want to spend a lot of time trying to force it to fit.

We can keep this as a long term goal indeed, and use that use-case to help the library improve until we obtain an output that is good enough. (Be aware that some do not agree on the automated approach: https://larlet.fr/david/blog/2016/specifications-apis/)

> @gabisurita proposed making Swagger stuff into a plugin.

I would strongly suggested to keep it in the same repo. Especially because the spec must evolve very closely with the code. And the tests should break if the spec does not match the resulting API (and vice-versa :)). It could be built-in plugin but what are the arguments in favor of running the API without it?

It does work – the caveat (as with most Flask extensions as far as I'm aware) is that if you use `init_app`, the app isn't bound to the extension instance (because you can call `init_app` multiple times with multiple apps), so instead you need to pass in the `app` when calling `add_resource`.

In other words, you'd do something like:

```python
api.add_resource('/widgets', WidgetView, app=app)
```

Since there's no app context while setting up routes, we can't infer the app from the current app, either.

First, I think a basic "check" is to try the Realsense SDK and see if you're still having issues. If so, there's an actual hardware problem and you should contact Intel support for warranty/support information. If that fixes it, then we've an actual librealsense bug.

Second, I think we should check if this bending is within the expected tolerances for shipped F200 units. @ddiakopoulos 

Third, I think this may just unfortunate, expected behavior. I don't believe we currently offer a method of re-calibrating the device in either the RSSDK or librealsense. The cameras are factory-calibrated, but there exist challenges to maintaining calibration and there's the possibility that certain cameras will fall out of calibration for some of the following

1. Mechanical impulses causing intrinsics or extrinsics changes, such as bending of the stiffener or shifting of the lens. The cameras are designed with a stiffener, but it's possible that mechanical impulses (e.g. dropping) might knock the device out of calibration.
2. Un-modeled temperature changes. We currently have a real-time loop running a temperature correction model that adjusts intrinsics. If you subject the device to strong or non-uniform temperature sources, this might cause this loop to fail. 
3. Reflection & multipath. If you have a highly reflective or specular environment, self-reflections in the scene might cause ambiguities. 
4. Un-modeled pressure changes. I won't talk about details, but our device can be sensitive to strong ambient pressure changes. There's sensors and logic to compensate for this but you never know.

My main worry is getting the implementation details right. One proposal is to make demo content available and then asking users to download that content locally and then setting up a content server. This could be cool - but how hard would that be to do? Setting up server for RPM files is easy: it requires just executing `python -m http.server 80` at the root of some directory tree. But what about docker and puppet, where the content server has to implement an API?

One option is to use Pulp itself as a content source. The big advantages here are that:

* We'd be dog-fooding.
* Pulp does support serving all these content types.

Bug there's some caveats:

* Pulp can be slow and buggy at times. (I'm not attacking anyone here! But let's be realistic.)
* Pulp can be hard to install.
* Pulp *can't* be installed on many platforms.
* Forcing the test suite user to set up a content server before testing Pulp raises the barrier to entry!

These cons can be mitigated if we have a Pulp server publicly (?) available to anyone who wants to use it.

Hi @mandx,

For using Graphene with `asyncio` you need to add `AsyncioExecutor` as the executor, like:

```python
from graphql.execution.executors.asyncio import AsyncioExecutor
from graphql import graphql

my_result = graphql(schema, '{ the query }', executor=AsyncioExecutor())
```

If you want the result to be awaitable you can also do:

```python
async def execute_query():
    my_result = await graphql(schema, '{ the query }', executor=AsyncioExecutor(), return_promise=True)
```

I was able to factorize my code a little bit by creating a new class where I define attributes description only once. Then I supply this new class as a parent class of the following classes:

- `class People(SQLAlchemyObjectType, PeopleAttribute)` : used for GraphQL queries
- `class CreatePersonInput(graphene.InputObjectType, PeopleAttribute)` : used to define inputs for GraphQL create mutation
- `class UpdatePersonInput(graphene.InputObjectType, PeopleAttribute)` : used to define inputs for GraphQL create mutation

Here is the code:

```python
# Create a generic class to mutualize description of people attributes for both queries and mutations
class PeopleAttribute:
    name = graphene.String(required=True, description="Name of the person.")
    height = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="Height of the person.")
    mass = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="Mass of the person.")
    hair_color = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="Hair color of the person.")
    skin_color = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="Skin color of the person.")
    eye_color = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="Eye color of the person.")
    birth_year = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="Birth year of the person.")
    gender = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="Gender of the person.")
    planet_id = graphene.ID(default_value="unknown", description="Global Id of the planet from which the person comes from.")
    url = graphene.String(default_value="unknown", description="URL of the person in the Star Wars API.")


class People(SQLAlchemyObjectType, PeopleAttribute):
    """People node."""

    class Meta:
        model = ModelPeople
        interfaces = (graphene.relay.Node,)


class CreatePersonInput(graphene.InputObjectType, PeopleAttribute):
    """Arguments to create a person."""
    pass


class UpdatePersonInput(graphene.InputObjectType, PeopleAttribute):
    """Arguments to update a person."""
    id = graphene.ID(required=True, description="Global Id of the person.")

```

New pushed commit with an updated commit log:

**[data-poll-manager] No parent, no data poll!**

The goal of this change is to ensure that data polls (IEEE 802.15.4
Data Request commands) are not sent when there is no parent (or parent
candidate) or the parent is not in valid/restoring states.

In both `DataPollManager` and `MeshForwarder` classes, new checks are
added to ensure that there is a valid parent before enqueuing/sending
a data poll message. Also in `MeshForwarder`, the MAC source and
destination addresses for a data poll frame are determined when
preparing the data poll message (in `GetDirectTransmission()`) instead
of from `SendPoll()` call.

This commit also adds two helper methods in `Mac::Frame` class for the
common operation of setting source/destination addresses on a frame using
a `Mac::Address` as input (which may contain either a short or an
extended address).

The IEEE 802.15.4 chips I work with typically can transition from a fully powered down state to receive/transmit states on the order of a millisecond.  The relative simplicity of IEEE 802.15.4 compared to WiFi allows for the faster state transition times.  In my experience, I've typically found that defining two different low-power/sleep states is not very valuable from a power perspective.  In practical scenarios, a sleepy have a polling period no smaller than tens of milliseconds - which makes going into the lowest power state the optimal solution.

That said, I think it would be helpful if we formally define the different radio states and their characteristics.  I have typically viewed the disabled state as not consuming any resources (memory, hardware muxes, etc.).  The sleep state does not have much of a power profile difference with the disabled state, but does potentially allocate/reserve resources necessary to guarantee receive/transmit when requested.

Regarding the particular scenario of NCP providing access to both OT and the radio, I think it might actually be useful to provide access to both simultaneously.  For example, being able to transmit raw IEEE 802.15.4 frames while OT is running can be useful for testing, debugging, and even basic multi-protocol support.

@patkan 
Here is a quick update.

I ended up relying on udev rules to differentiate my printers. I did not modify python-escpos at all.

It turns out that *some* models of Epson thermal printers (e.g. TM-T88V) expose a unique serial number over the USB connection, while other models (e.g. TM-T60) don't. It's possible to write udev rules accordingly.

# Printers that expose a unique serial number
In the shell:
```
$ udevadm info -q all -p /sys/bus/usb/devices/1-1.5

Udevadm info starts with the device specified by the devpath and then
walks up the chain of parent devices. It prints for every device
found, all possible attributes in the udev rules key format.
A rule to match, can be composed by the attributes of the device
and the attributes from one single parent device.

  looking at device '/devices/platform/soc/3f980000.usb/usb1/1-1/1-1.5':
    KERNEL=="1-1.5"
    SUBSYSTEM=="usb"
    DRIVER=="usb"
[...]
    ATTR{manufacturer}=="EPSON"
[...]
    ATTR{product}=="TM-T88V"
[...]
    ATTR{serial}=="4E334A<censored>"
[...]
```

So I can write a udev rule like this:
```
SUBSYSTEM=="usbmisc", ATTRS{manufacturer}=="EPSON", ATTRS{product}=="TM-T88V", ATTRS{serial}="4E334A<censored>", SYMLINK+="epson_tm_t88v_1"
```

This creates a /dev/epson_tm_t88v_1 symlink that I can open using `printer.File("/dev/epson_tm_t88v_1")`.

# Printers that don't expose a unique serial number
I have to rely on the fact that the numbering of USB ports and devices is deterministic and stable when the Raspberry Pi 3 reboots: a printer plugged into a particular built-in USB port will always have the same sysfs path.

So I can write udev rules like this:
```
SUBSYSTEM=="usbmisc", KERNELS=="1-1.3", ATTRS{manufacturer}=="EPSON", ATTRS{product}=="TM-T60", SYMLINK+="epson_tm_t60_1"
```

This will create a /dev/epson_tm_t60_1 symlink that I can open using `printer.File("/dev/epson_tm_t60_1")`.

This issue happens in Demag GUI when a file has multiple rows for the same item.  This can cause problems in MagIC 3.  This line in get_data_info:

    data_er_samples = samp_data2.T.to_dict()

is the problem.  Unlike a dataframe index, a dictionary can't have non-unique keys, so data is lost.

For example, when this abridged samples table is turned into a dictionary:

          azimuth
    sample              
    hz05a      240 
    hz05a     
    hz05b      190 
    hz05b     

It could look like:

    {'hz05a': {'azimuth': ''},
     'hz05b': {'azimuth': ''}

or:

    {'hz05a': {'azimuth': 240},
     'hz05b': {'azimuth': 190}}

There is no way to predict which.

One solution would be to propagate certain columns values in each table (whatever is important for Demag GUI, like orientation headers in the samples table).  This could definitely slow things down, though, especially with large tables.  


   samples.front_and_backfill(['azimuth'])

            azimuth
    sample         
    hz05a     240.0
    hz05a     240.0
    hz05b     190.0
    hz05b     190.0

Then it won't matter which row gets turned into a dictionary, at least with respect to azimuth.  We could choose a list of important columns, and make sure the values in those columns are applied to every row.

Alternately, we could try dropping non-important rows, possibly by method code, before turning the DataFrame into a dictionary.

Demag GUI people, any thoughts on this?  @Swanson-Hysell, @Caoimhinmg, @ltauxe?

Okay, so closing the issue was the right decision. :) Further investigation has uncovered that in fact it is NOT PyFFTW that is the culprit... it is Numerical Expressions. We are using Numexpr to do a very large array operation... and it turns out that, randomly, Numexpr will return a zeroed array rather than the correct result. No error is thrown... just a zero array of the appropriate size. So PyFFTW is off the hook!  Please forgive me for the error; initial testing indicated otherwise.

I think the answer is yes, but I have one clarifying question: which part of `dask-ml` are you using? Dask-ml doesn't have a custom pipeline object (yet), we just re-use `sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline`. Are you using `dask_ml.model_selection.GridSearchCV`, or something else?

There's another more basic advantage to implementing a pipeline in Dask: repeated function calls will not be re-computed. This is advantageous in a hyperparameter search, especially when the initial steps are expensive (e.g., with a CountVectorizer which computes n-grams).

Are you working with dask or NumPy arrays? IOW, is your data larger than memory?

`sklearn.model_selection.cross_validate` and `learning_curve` should work already in a `with joblib.backend('dask')` context manager for in-memory datasets, though I haven't tested it.

I have the mechanism for doing the upgrades mostly implemented in the `os-auto-update-system` branch. Making use of it to stop using Python 2.6 will come in a later PR.

I'm currently a little stuck on the following problem though:

I suspect most users who have gotten `certbot-auto` to run automatically from cron or a systemd service are running the script as root. This is because otherwise the script will try to run `sudo`/`su` which doesn't work (by default) in cron or systemd services. This means that Certbot is installed at `/root/.local/share/letsencrypt`.

Additionally, I suspect most users don't use `sudo`/`su` themselves when they run the script manually. Until a very recent change to our website, we never told them to because `certbot-auto` runs `sudo`/`su` as necessary internally. (The fact that we now tell users to run `sudo certbot-auto` is an unattended side-effect of another change.) This (coupled with the fact that `sudo` preserves `$HOME` by default on many systems) means that Certbot is installed at `~/.local/share/letsencrypt` where `~` is not `/root`.

Because of this, I suspect most `certbot-auto` users who are using cron/systemd have two Certbot installations. Why does this matter here? Well when you start getting root e-mail from cron/systemd saying that Certbot has an upgrade available but needs your attention, you'll log in and run `certbot-auto` and upgrade, but the `root` installation of `certbot-auto` has no idea that this has happened and continues to try and get your attention.

Potential mitigations I've thought of are:

1. Put `certbot-auto-bootstrap-version.txt` in one of Certbot's configuration directories, but this is much more visible to the user and breaks if the user changes directories or deletes their configuration to start over.
2. Change the default installation location of `certbot-auto` to work around this problem. We can do this, but it's non-trivial code to write to ensure upgrades continue to work and will break users using `venv/bin` directly (we know there are at least a few).
3. Try and tell the sysadmin which user to run `certbot-auto` as. I haven't come up with a reliable way to do this.

Your self-proclaimed logical spaghetti seems a rather complete solution,
but it does feel a bit complicated. Wouldn't a solution like the
following be easier and less intrusive?

Alternative solution I
----------------------

1. if no `--log-target` or `--logs-dir` specified:
    1. default to `--log-target /var/log/letsencrypt/` (or
       `/var/log/certbot/` if you prefer).
2. if `--logs-dir` is specified:
    1. deprecation warning,
    2. continue as if the value was specified for `--log-target` (maybe
       append `/`?).
3. if `--log-target` is specified (or obtained through `--logs-dir` as
   noted above):
    1. if `--log-target == "stderr"`, log to `stderr` (like the default
       action),
    2. if `--log-target == "syslog"`, require `--syslog-facility` to be
       specified and log to syslog,
    3. if `--log-target` points at a directory (maybe even require
       target ending in a `/`?), log to specified directory (create if
       necessary) and do rotation (old behaviour) unless
       `--no-log-rotation` is specified,
    4. else: log to the file specified (create if necessary) and don't
       rotate, since we only know about one file.

Alternative first step to alternative solution I
------------------------------------------------

Which I would personally prefer, but has more effect on current
behaviour:

1. if no `--log-target` or `--logs-dir` specified:
    1. warning: `"default log target will change to stderr in the next
       version"`,
    2. continue as if `--log-target /var/log/letsencrypt/` was specified
       (old location + behaviour),
    3. in the future: default to `--log-target stderr`.

This last idea is obviously (even more) breaking, and you guys may not
agree since it also may make it harder for people to find the right data
to provide in case necessary. The idea behind this is that `certbot` by
default is a command line tool, which makes logging to a file by default
illogical to me. Default logging to a file is something I'd expect of
automated tools (and for that purpose it can be configured - but even
then things like `cron` handle `stderr` output fine) or
daemons/services.

Notes:
- I proposed the use of `last_received_time` because if we are actively exchanging transfers I don't see the *need* for a Ping message.
  - so, while we are running this [loop](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/f6745762d8a35e1e4f98c1704e1aae689ae52339/raiden/raiden_protocol.py#L94) or [not queue.empty()](http://www.gevent.org/gevent.queue.html#gevent.queue.Queue.empty) we don't need pings. Note: like `Ack` I don't think `Ping` needs to be in the queue because there are no ordering constraints.
- We don't actually need a `Task` for waiting, using `peek(timeout=ping_timeout)` for each message queue and checking for `None` should be suficient [1](http://www.gevent.org/gevent.queue.html#gevent.queue.Queue.peek).

You could call it a sliding window. Let's say you're taking the input and labels:

```
a a a a a a a a a
0 0 1 0 0 1 0 0 1
```

Every third symbol in the input receives a "1", otherwise the output is "0". Obviously so long as we have two states of history, a markov model can learn this easily. It's easy to imagine how the LSTM would work for this too.

The CNN isn't as intuitive. Consider the first layer:

    inputs = concat(prev_input[i-1], prev_input[i], prev_input[i+1]) 

If the previous input is the original string, the representation for this first layer is the same almost everywhere --- it's only different at the beginning and end. So we can distinguish three "states" here: beginning, middle and end:
    
```
a a a a a a a a a
b m m m m m m e
0 0 1 0 0 1 0 0 1
```

But now when we do the next layer, we're making some progress. After the next convolution, we could learn new states for second and paenultimate (second last):

```
a a a a a a a a a
b m m m m m m e
b s m m m m p e
0 0 1 0 0 1 0 0 1
```

Now we finally have some unambiguous features. Our input-to-output map for the next layer looks like this:

```
(_, b, s): 0
(b, s, m): 0
(s, m, m): 1
(m, m, m): 0
(m, m, p): 1
(m, p, e): 0
(p, e, _): 0
```

Each layer of depth allows information to flow from two more tokens (one on either side) --- so it increases the window size by 1.

@marky1991 I introspect the attributes in order to generate fixtures. SubFactory means that there's a factory behind it. I think Dict is a factory that has a dict as a model. Dict isn't really a subfactory that has a factory of yet another factory that is just an indirection to come to the dict or an orderdereddict.

dict_factory as a parameter makes very little sense to me, model as a parameter would do more sense. Which dict you want to use. Honestly ordereddict doesn't make a lot of sense to me, because once you iterate over it's items it is no longer a dict. Dict is an interface with an index operator [].

Dict(ordered=True) or Dict(model_class=ordereddict) would make more sense than being a SubFactory.
What if I pass Dict(factory_class=List)? I think it is an architectural mistake to make Dict IS A SubFactory, which broke a user space.

The question is how far did it go and can it be undone?

I have a suggestion on the design of the external API for this support:
1. The operation for opening the message channel should be a method on the `Partition` and `Lpar` classes, respectively, that returns an object of a new `MessageChannel` class.
2. The `MessageChannel` class has a method `get_next()` that waits for the next message on the JMS notification topic for the channel, and returns it to its caller once the next message has been received.

This way, the whole area of multithreading/multiprocessing/eventing/notification can be done in any way by the user, and the zhmcclient package does not need to select a particular multiprocessing or eventing model.

This is a good point, it looks like we may not be handling all cases at the moment.

In your example, the **driver script** starts a **local scheduler**, and the local scheduler starts a number of **worker processes**.

If the driver exits gracefully, it will send a kill signal to the local scheduler (via `services.cleanup`), which should run the local scheduler's cleanup handler, which kills all of the workers. The relevant lines of code are

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/c26c7553bcb0c181e196a0b18dc647791473fc16/python/ray/worker.py#L1365

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/c26c7553bcb0c181e196a0b18dc647791473fc16/src/local_scheduler/local_scheduler.cc#L154

Now, it looks like in the situation where I send a kill signal to the driver script, the local scheduler does not die (and therefore does not clean up the workers). In this case, we want to cause the local scheduler to die when the driver exits.

Note that this should only be relevant in the case where you start ray with `ray.init()` on a single machine. In the cluster case you don't want the workers (or any of the processes) to exit when a single driver fails.

What you're missing is the hidden state input and output of the LSTM. So a more complete diagram looks like this:
```
observation (o_t), hidden state (h_t)

                  ---------\
o_0, h_0         /   o_1, h_1                  (inputs)
 |    |         /     |     |
 |    |        /      |     |
model |       /      model  |
  \   /      /         \   /
   cell     /         cell -----.......
     |     /             |
    a_0, h_1            a_1, h_2               (outputs)

```

So the observation is first processed by the model, then the outputs of that and the LSTM hidden state are passed to the LSTM cell. The LSTM cell emits the new model outputs, and also the next hidden state.

# Background
## Graph Computing
We have graph computing scenario based on Ray. Ray can be used as the backend for the continuous graph computing requests. The requests have the following characteristics:
1. In the scenario of graph computing, object number grows exponentially. If unused memory is not release in time, the memory will be a concern.
2. There are many requests at the same time and there are continual requests all the time.
3. The computation costs for different requests are different. It is not guaranteed that the finishing order is the same as the creation order.

Currently, the Plasma Store will evict the objects according to creation time when the memory is not enough. However, this mechanism is not enough. For one thing, our scenario needs large amount of memory, so it won't take long to use up the memory with incoming requests. For another, if an object is evicted according to creation time while it is still in use for later tasks, there will be a reconstruction of the object, which will affect the performance. Since we have the knowledge which requests are finished and won't be used in the future, we can release the resource safely. 
## Streaming Computing
In the further evolution, Ray will support stream computing. The life cycle for streaming objects are different. Even the best automatic garbage collecion may not be enough. We need to provide the elaborate memory management like RAII to enable users to manage object life cycle according to their needs.
# Solution
We introduce a concept of Batch and BatchId (`int64_t`). It is an identifier for a set of Ray tasks and Ray objects related to one high-level request/job concept. After the high-level request/job finished, there is a new API in Ray for users to release all the resources from GCS and Plasma according to one BatchId. 
# Implementation Design
## API Level
```python
# APIs:
# This function can be called after Ray.init().
# If this function is called, there is a batch concept in this Ray Driver. 
# If not, Ray will work as normal.
ray.start_batch(batch_id) 

# This function can be called by the users 
# if they think all their resources are not needed in the future.
ray.end_batch(batch_id, need_gc = true)  

###########################################################
# Scenario 1: Normal Mode
@ray.remote
def f():
    time.sleep(1)
    return 1

ray.init()
results = ray.get([f.remote() for i in range(4)])

# Scenario 2: Batch Mode
@ray.remote
def f():
    time.sleep(1)
    return 1

ray.init()
batchId = ray.start_batch()
results = ray.get([f.remote() for i in range(4)])
# Some other work...
ray.end_batch(batchId, need_gc = true)
```
The Java API can be design in a similar way. 
## ObjectId Bits
Currently, the lowest 32 bits are used for Object Index. The full higher 128 bits are used for TaskId. We can further split the 128 bits into two parts. The highest 64 bits can be used for BatchId. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19584326/41425077-3f5a5296-7032-11e8-91cb-21f269db2ca5.png)
## Backend Support
### GCS Support
There will be a GCS table to record the ObjectIds and TaskIds under one BatchId if the Batch mode is enable. The table name is "GC:${batchId}". Every objectId and taskId will be pushed into this table. After garbage collection is called with batchId, the GCS will release all the objectIds and taskIds under this table.
### Plasma Support
Node manager will subscribe the delete message of the "GC:${batchId}" from GCS. If GCS has deleted the garbage collection table, the node manager will trigger a garbage collection request to Plasma. Plasma will go through all the objectIds and delete the objects under the requested batchId.

Yeah, that's expected since the Ray schedulers assigns the full GPU to each trial (if you request 2 gpus, the trial has exclusive access to those GPUs until it completes).

Support for fractional GPUs is not yet implemented: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/402

As a workaround you can manage GPU scheduling yourself (request 0 gpus), and just set CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES etc. manually. You can also tweak the CPU resource request to avoid overloading the GPUs, e.g. even if only need 1 CPU you can request more CPUs so that Ray will not schedule too many concurrent trials on the two GPUs of the node.

I agree with what I think is the crux of your issue with this proposal, and suggest a change that fixes it in the last paragraph. For the most part my proposal is the same.

There are a few reasons for components to know about tracers a priori:

- The component knows whether it handles tracers or not when you write it. Having the component expose interfaces to deal with tracers tells you about this.
- We want tracers to be just any other quantity in state. This means components must request the tracer quantities in their input properties, and list them in their output or tendency properties. This requires telling the component about the tracer names and units (along with the dimensions it applies equally to all tracers).
- Different components may have different quantities that they consider to be tracers. For a specific example, a higher-order turbulence closure will not consider higher-order turbulent moments to be tracers, but may want its higher-order turbulent moments to be considered tracers by the horizontal advection scheme. This makes it impossible to have a single array of tracers that is used by all components.

In order to satisfy separation of concerns, the above has the component handle only its own responsibilities (exposing the correct input and output properties), and passes on all the actual work for making and unpacking tracer arrays to this new object. It is specifically because of separation of concerns that there must be a new component here - we have a new responsibility, which is combining quantity arrays into tracer arrays and vice-versa, so we need a TracerPacker.

We can't have tracers be their own quantity in the state mainly because the set of tracers is not guaranteed to be universal across components. Some components will want them as normal inputs. This requires that the State be a lot more complicated than a dict, as it has to pack and unpack tracers. And then we *still* have the problem that each component needs to know *which* tracers it's actually handling as tracers.

We could put the tracer handling functionality into the base classes in some way. For example, we could have a property allows_tracers which if True will result in the base __call__ and __init__ methods doing all this tracer packing and unpacking stuff, so that array_call just sees a quantity "tracers". Then when you're actually writing components, the amount of boilerplate code related to tracers is limited to one line (maybe two since it also needs to specify the dims of the tracers).

Now, the part I don't like about the current proposal is having to pass in the tracer names to each component. It seems like a lot of configuration boilerplate for the user. Perhaps it would be best if there is a central registry of tracers. If you remove any quantities that a component uses as normal inputs, you have the list of tracer names specific to that component. The base classes could get that specific list of tracer names and handle packing and unpacking. It could *also* allow the user to override this default list using explicitly passed names as previously proposed (for if they're doing something wonky). I'd have the base classes do this within specific methods, so that component writers can easily overwrite them if they so wish. This doesn't work for the other way of doing things (having a "tracer" quantity in the state) because the set of tracers used for each component is still unique. This way still allows components to explicitly list tracer quantity names in their input and output properties.

> I'm struggling to tell apart TEXT_ELEMENT, COPY, and COPY_TEXT. In particular because TEXT_ELEMENT is allowed next to COPY, but COPY_TEXT isn't? They were both allowed and I actually realized that given how CONCAT works, TEXT_ELEMENT was redundant. I removed it. To answer your other questions I'd like to take a step back and summarize how transforms are currently designed. I'm open to changing this, btw. If this explanation is helpful I'll put it in a README, too. We need to consider both the input and the output. ### Input The transforms ending with `_TEXT` take raw text (`type() == str`) as input. In majority of cases this text comes from legacy translations which needs to be defined using the SOURCE operation. ``` COPY_TEXT(SOURCE('file.dtd', 'hello')) ``` For convenience, the versions without `_TEXT` take the `path` and the `key` which will be looked up in the legacy translations stored in the current `MergeContext`. ``` COPY('file.dtd', 'hello') ``` Sometimes, it's still useful to use the `*_TEXT` version of a transform. You may want to put a hardcoded text into the migrated translation (e.g. `` and `` when multiple translations become a single one with a DOM overlay); or you may want to combine PLURALS with REPLACE_TEXT. In this case, each variant of the plural string will be run through a REPLACE_TEXT operation. Those variants are strings, so a `REPLACE(path, key, …)` isn't suitable for them. ### Output Most transforms evaluate to `FTL.Pattern` so that they can be used as values of messages, attributes and variants. This is by far the most common scenario: ``` msg = FTL.Message( FTL.Identifier('hello'), value=COPY('file.dtd', 'hello'), ) ``` In case of variants, the PLURALS transform slices the original translation into multiple strings, one per each plural category. REPLACE_TEXT is run on each string and evaluates to an `FTL.Pattern` which is suitable as a value of an `FTL.SelectExpression` variant. ``` msg = FTL.Message( FTL.Identifier('delete-all'), value=PLURALS( 'file.dtd', 'deleteAll', EXTERNAL_ARGUMENT('num'), lambda variant: REPLACE_TEXT( variant, { '#1': EXTERNAL_ARGUMENT('num') } ) ) ) ``` For convenience, I chose to add two transforms which do not evaluate to `FTL.Pattern` and thus cannot be used as values of messages, attributes and variants. These are MESSAGE_REFERENCE and EXTERNAL_ARGUMENT. Instead of typing: ``` FTL.ExternalArgument( id=FTL.Identifier('foo') ) ``` …you can simply type: ``` EXTERNAL_ARGUMENT('foo') ``` They can be used as selectors in the PLURAL transform, as replacements in REPLACE and REPLACE_TEXT and as elements to CONCAT. ---- I'm looking for a good balance between consistency with the Fluent AST and convenience. I hope this explanation was helpful. Now, what should we call all these transforms? :)

@sudoguy @ohld I have made some changes to auto_post.py and know the captions are loaded from text files. User needs to obey from this naming convention which the name of the files should get started by numbers so the caption of "1 First pic.jpg" will be inside "1 caption.txt". The name of the files are not important but the numbers are. 

`import time
import sys
import os
import yaml
import glob

sys.path.append(os.path.join(sys.path[0], '../../'))
from instabot import Bot

posted_pic_list = []
try:
    with open('pics.txt', 'r') as f:
        posted_pic_list = f.read().splitlines()
except:
    posted_pic_list = []

timeout = 24 * 60 * 60 

bot = Bot()
bot.login()

while True:
    pics = glob.glob("./pics/*.jpg")
    pics = sorted(pics)
    try:
        for pic in pics:
            if pic in posted_pic_list:
                continue

            path = "./pics"
            for root, dirs, files in os.walk(path):
                jpgs =[]
                texts=[]
                for name in sorted(files):
                    if name.endswith(".jpg"):
                        jpgs.append(name)
                    if name.endswith(".txt"):
                        with open(path +"/" + name, "r") as caption_file:
                            caption = caption_file.read()
                            texts.append(caption)

                for i in range(0,len(jpgs)):
                    print("upload: " + texts[i])
                    mypic = path + "/"+jpgs[i]
                    bot.uploadPhoto(mypic, caption=texts[i])
                    if bot.LastResponse.status_code != 200:
                        print(bot.LastResponse)
                        # snd msg
                        break

                    if not pic in posted_pic_list:
                        posted_pic_list.append(pic)
                        with open('pics.txt', 'a') as f:
                            f.write(pic + "\n")

            time.sleep(timeout)

    except Exception as e:
        print(str(e))
    time.sleep(60)
`

Setting `rate=1.0` means that time passes at one virtual second per real second. It *doesn't* change the epoch or anything like that, and we can't provide a way to convert `trio.current_time()` to a calendar time – they're just on different scales. Which doesn't mean your use case isn't important, it just means we should think about it carefully :-).

My suggestion would be: in normal use, use a wall clock, like `time.time` or whatever. If you need to sleep until a given wall clock time... well, solving this problem is actually quite tricky because of things like time changes, but there are some hints as to how to solve it properly if you follow the link in #173, or a first-pass hack would be to compute a time delta using POSIX times, and then pretend that that's something you can pass to `trio.sleep`. (It'll work well enough most of the time.) And make sure this wall clock time stuff is encapsulated in some object, similar to Trio's clock.

Then, for testing, you can swap in a fake version of your clock, and you make the fake clock track an *offset* between what the Trio monotonic clock says, and whatever fake wall time you want to pretend it is. So if you say "okay fake clock, it's currently 2018-01-07 23:37:10.1349Z", then it converts that to a POSIX time, calls `trio.current_time()` to get Trio's idea of the time, and then stashes that offset somewhere. Later when you ask your fake clock what time it is, it does `trio.current_time() + self._stored_offset`. That way, for testing purposes you could combine `trio.testing.MockClock` with your fake clock, and all the magic -- including autojumping! -- would Just Work.

And you'd probably also want some other tools in your fake clock (or in a second fake clock implementation) for simulating stuff like DST transitions and leap seconds and NTP time updates.

One consideration I hadn't fully realized when writing my comments above: if you have multiple tasks using the same stream, calling `send_all` directly can be problematic, because as soon as any `send_all` is cancelled, your whole stream is corrupted and can't be used. So I guess that's another reason why you need to think carefully when sharing a stream object between multiple tasks, and having an implicit lock wouldn't actually fix all your problems.

Hi, are you using `docker run ...` to start LocalStack? The problem in that case is that environment variables in your local terminal session are *not* automatically passed into the Docker container.

Therefore, the recommended way of starting LocalStack is using the CLI:
```
pip install localstack
LAMBDA_EXECUTOR=docker localstack start
```
Alternatively, if you want to stick with `docker run ...`, you can use the `-e` flag:
```
docker run -e LAMBDA_EXECUTOR=docker ...
```
Hope that helps.

been doing some more reading on this. Actually what we would want is specifically an async version of D4PG.  This looks the gold standard these days.
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1801.00690.pdf

Here's the paper outlining it:
https://openreview.net/pdf?id=SyZipzbCb

Essentially, predicting scaler Q-values is a thing of the past. We really want to predict distributions instead of average/expected returns. D4PG would let us both do asyncronous training, and also use the distributional form of the bellman equation, which has a ton of advantages.
